A manufacturer claims that \(p=0.20\) of parts need rework. In a random sample of \(n=250\), \(x=65\) need rework. Test \(H_0:p=0.20\) against \(H_1:p\ne0.20\) at level \(0.05\).

The sample proportion is \(\hat p=65/250=0.260\). Under \(H_0\), the standard error is \[\sqrt{\frac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}=\sqrt{\frac{0.20(0.80)}{250}}=0.02530.\] The test statistic is \[z=\frac{0.260-0.200}{0.02530}=2.37.\] For a two-sided test, \(P\text{-value}=2P(Z\ge2.37)\approx0.0178\). Since \(0.0178<0.05\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that the rework proportion differs from 0.20.

Hypothesis tests use the standard error implied by the null value (p_0), because the null distribution is what produces the P-value.
thought_text: Hypothesis tests use the standard error implied by the null value (p_0), because the null distribution is what produces the P-value.

question_text: A website historically converts \(p_0=0.12\) of visitors into buyers. After a redesign, \(38\) of \(400\) visitors buy. Test whether the redesign decreased the conversion rate using \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: The hypotheses are \(H_0:p=0.12\) and \(H_1:p<0.12\). The observed proportion is \(\hat p=38/400=0.095\). Under the null, \[\text{SE}_0=\sqrt{\frac{0.12(0.88)}{400}}=0.01625,\qquad z=\frac{0.095-0.120}{0.01625}=-1.54.\] The lower-tail P-value is \(P(Z\le-1.54)\approx0.062\). Since \(0.062>0.05\), do not reject \(H_0\). The data do not give significant evidence of a decrease at the 5% level.
text_for_embedding:
A website historically converts \(p_0=0.12\) of visitors into buyers. After a redesign, \(38\) of \(400\) visitors buy. Test whether the redesign decreased the conversion rate using \(\alpha=0.05\).

The hypotheses are \(H_0:p=0.12\) and \(H_1:p<0.12\). The observed proportion is \(\hat p=38/400=0.095\). Under the null, \[\text{SE}_0=\sqrt{\frac{0.12(0.88)}{400}}=0.01625,\qquad z=\frac{0.095-0.120}{0.01625}=-1.54.\] The lower-tail P-value is \(P(Z\le-1.54)\approx0.062\). Since \(0.062>0.05\), do not reject \(H_0\). The data do not give significant evidence of a decrease at the 5% level.

The sign of (z) matters for a one-sided test. Do not double the P-value unless the alternative is two-sided.
thought_text: The sign of (z) matters for a one-sided test. Do not double the P-value unless the alternative is two-sided.

question_text: A 90% confidence interval for a population proportion is reported as \([0.41,0.49]\). Give a correct interpretation and identify one incorrect interpretation.
solution_text: A correct interpretation is: the procedure used to construct the interval would capture the fixed population proportion \(p\) in 90% of repeated random samples. One incorrect interpretation is: there is a 90% probability that \(p\) is between 0.41 and 0.49 after this interval has been computed. In the usual frequentist interpretation, \(p\) is fixed and the random quantity is the interval before observing the data.
text_for_embedding:

A correct interpretation is: the procedure used to construct the interval would capture the fixed population proportion \(p\) in 90% of repeated random samples. One incorrect interpretation is: there is a 90% probability that \(p\) is between 0.41 and 0.49 after this interval has been computed. In the usual frequentist interpretation, \(p\) is fixed and the random quantity is the interval before observing the data.

A confidence interval is about a population parameter, not the percentage of future observations that must fall in the numerical interval.
thought_text: A confidence interval is about a population parameter, not the percentage of future observations that must fall in the numerical interval.

question_text: How large should \(n\) be to estimate a population proportion with 95% confidence and margin of error at most \(0.04\), if no preliminary estimate of \(p\) is available?
solution_text: Without a preliminary estimate, use the conservative value \(p^*=1/2\), which maximizes \(p(1-p)\). The planning formula is \[n\ge\frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2p^*(1-p^*)}{E^2}.\] With \(z_{0.025}=1.96\), \(E=0.04\), and \(p^*=0.5\), \[n\ge\frac{1.96^2(0.25)}{0.04^2}=600.25.\] Round up to \(n=601\).
text_for_embedding:
How large should \(n\) be to estimate a population proportion with 95% confidence and margin of error at most \(0.04\), if no preliminary estimate of \(p\) is available?

Without a preliminary estimate, use the conservative value \(p^*=1/2\), which maximizes \(p(1-p)\). The planning formula is \[n\ge\frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2p^*(1-p^*)}{E^2}.\] With \(z_{0.025}=1.96\), \(E=0.04\), and \(p^*=0.5\), \[n\ge\frac{1.96^2(0.25)}{0.04^2}=600.25.\] Round up to \(n=601\).

Rounding down would make the planned margin slightly larger than allowed. Always round required sample sizes up.
thought_text: Rounding down would make the planned margin slightly larger than allowed. Always round required sample sizes up.

question_text: In testing \(H_0:p=0.30\) against \(H_1:p>0.30\), a study reports \(P\text{-value}=0.018\). Interpret this P-value in context.
solution_text: If the true population proportion were \(0.30\), the probability of observing a sample proportion at least as large as the one observed, just by random sampling variation, would be about \(0.018\). It is not the probability that \(H_0\) is true, and it is not the probability that \(p>0.30\).

If the true population proportion were \(0.30\), the probability of observing a sample proportion at least as large as the one observed, just by random sampling variation, would be about \(0.018\). It is not the probability that \(H_0\) is true, and it is not the probability that \(p>0.30\).

A P-value is computed assuming the null hypothesis, not assigning probability to the null hypothesis.
thought_text: A P-value is computed assuming the null hypothesis, not assigning probability to the null hypothesis.

question_text: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(n,p)\) with \(n=200\) and \(p=0.35\). State the approximate sampling distribution of \(\hat p=X/n\), and compute its mean and standard deviation.
solution_text: For large \(n\), \(\hat p\) is approximately normal: \[\hat p\approx N\left(p,\frac{p(1-p)}{n}\right)=N\left(0.35,\frac{0.35(0.65)}{200}\right).\] The mean is \(0.35\), and the standard deviation is \(\sqrt{0.35(0.65)/200}=0.0337\).
text_for_embedding:

For large \(n\), \(\hat p\) is approximately normal: \[\hat p\approx N\left(p,\frac{p(1-p)}{n}\right)=N\left(0.35,\frac{0.35(0.65)}{200}\right).\] The mean is \(0.35\), and the standard deviation is \(\sqrt{0.35(0.65)/200}=0.0337\).

The binomial count (X) has variance (np(1-p)), while the sample proportion (X/n) has variance (p(1-p)/n).
thought_text: The binomial count (X) has variance (np(1-p)), while the sample proportion (X/n) has variance (p(1-p)/n).

question_text: For a two-sided level \(0.01\) large-sample test of \(H_0:p=p_0\), what critical values of the \(z\)-statistic are used? State the rejection rule.
solution_text: For a two-sided level \(0.01\) test, the total tail area \(0.01\) is split into \(0.005\) in each tail. Thus the critical value is \(z_{0.005}\approx2.576\). Reject \(H_0\) when \(|z|>2.576\), equivalently when \(z<-2.576\) or \(z>2.576\).
text_for_embedding:

For a two-sided level \(0.01\) test, the total tail area \(0.01\) is split into \(0.005\) in each tail. Thus the critical value is \(z_{0.005}\approx2.576\). Reject \(H_0\) when \(|z|>2.576\), equivalently when \(z<-2.576\) or \(z>2.576\).

Do not use (z_{0.01}=2.326) for a two-sided 1% test; that would put 1% in each tail.
thought_text: Do not use (z_{0.01}=2.326) for a two-sided 1% test; that would put 1% in each tail.

question_text: Explain the difference between \(p\), \(\hat p\), and \(p_0\) in one-proportion inference.
solution_text: \(p\) is the unknown population proportion, the fixed parameter we want to estimate or test. \(\hat p=X/n\) is the sample proportion computed from the data, so it varies from sample to sample. \(p_0\) is the specific value of \(p\) asserted by the null hypothesis in a test. In a confidence interval, \(\hat p\) is used to estimate \(p\). In a test of \(H_0:p=p_0\), the null standard error uses \(p_0\).
text_for_embedding:
Explain the difference between \(p\), \(\hat p\), and \(p_0\) in one-proportion inference.

\(p\) is the unknown population proportion, the fixed parameter we want to estimate or test. \(\hat p=X/n\) is the sample proportion computed from the data, so it varies from sample to sample. \(p_0\) is the specific value of \(p\) asserted by the null hypothesis in a test. In a confidence interval, \(\hat p\) is used to estimate \(p\). In a test of \(H_0:p=p_0\), the null standard error uses \(p_0\).

Many arithmetic mistakes in this chapter come from using the right formula with the wrong version of (p).
thought_text: Many arithmetic mistakes in this chapter come from using the right formula with the wrong version of (p).

question_text: A poll of \(n=900\) voters finds \(x=477\) support a proposal. Construct a 90% confidence interval for the support proportion.
text_for_embedding:
A poll of \(n=900\) voters finds \(x=477\) support a proposal. Construct a 90% confidence interval for the support proportion.

\(\hat p=477/900=0.530\). The standard error is \[\sqrt{\frac{0.53(0.47)}{900}}=0.01664.\] For 90% confidence, \(z_{0.05}=1.645\). The margin is \(1.645(0.01664)=0.0274\). Thus \[p\in[0.503,0.557].\]

A 90% interval is narrower than a 95% interval from the same data because it uses a smaller critical value.
thought_text: A 90% interval is narrower than a 95% interval from the same data because it uses a smaller critical value.

question_text: A course coordinator expects at most 15% of students to miss a weekly quiz. In one week, \(52\) of \(280\) students miss it. Test \(H_0:p=0.15\) against \(H_1:p>0.15\) at level \(0.05\).
solution_text: The sample proportion is \(\hat p=52/280=0.1857\). Under the null, \[\text{SE}_0=\sqrt{\frac{0.15(0.85)}{280}}=0.02134.\] The statistic is \[z=\frac{0.1857-0.1500}{0.02134}=1.67.\] The upper-tail P-value is \(P(Z\ge1.67)\approx0.047\). Since \(0.047<0.05\), reject \(H_0\) at the 5% level. There is marginal evidence that the missed-quiz proportion exceeds 15%.

The sample proportion is \(\hat p=52/280=0.1857\). Under the null, \[\text{SE}_0=\sqrt{\frac{0.15(0.85)}{280}}=0.02134.\] The statistic is \[z=\frac{0.1857-0.1500}{0.02134}=1.67.\] The upper-tail P-value is \(P(Z\ge1.67)\approx0.047\). When the wording says 'exceeds,' 'greater than,' or 'more than,' the P-value is an upper-tail probability.

question_text: A pilot survey estimates \(p\approx0.18\). How many observations are needed for a 95% confidence interval with margin of error at most \(0.03\), using the pilot estimate?
text_for_embedding:
A pilot survey estimates \(p\approx0.18\). How many observations are needed for a 95% confidence interval with margin of error at most \(0.03\), using the pilot estimate?

Use \[n\ge\frac{z_{\alpha/2}^2p^*(1-p^*)}{E^2}\] with \(p^*=0.18\), \(E=0.03\), and \(z_{0.025}=1.96\). \[n\ge\frac{1.96^2(0.18)(0.82)}{0.03^2}=630.02.\] Round up to \(n=631\).

A pilot estimate can reduce the planned sample size compared with conservative (p=0.5), but it relies on the pilot being credible.
thought_text: A pilot estimate can reduce the planned sample size compared with conservative (p=0.5), but it relies on the pilot being credible.

question_text: For the design in Q13, compute the conservative sample size using \(p^*=0.5\). Compare it with the pilot-based value \(n=631\).
solution_text: Using \(p^*=0.5\), \[n\ge\frac{1.96^2(0.25)}{0.03^2}=1067.11.\] Thus the conservative design requires \(n=1068\). This is larger than \(631\) because \(p(1-p)\) is maximized at \(p=0.5\). The conservative design protects the margin of error even when no reliable preliminary estimate is available.

The phrase 'conservative' means safe for all possible (p), not smaller or less cautious.

question_text: For \(H_0:p=0.04\), a planned large-sample test will use \(n=180\). Check the normal-approximation condition under the null and state whether the usual \(z\)-test is appropriate.
solution_text: For a hypothesis test, check the expected counts under the null value \(p_0\): \[np_0=180(0.04)=7.2,\qquad n(1-p_0)=180(0.96)=172.8.\] The success count is below 10, so the normal approximation is questionable. The usual large-sample \(z\)-test is not well justified; an exact binomial test would be safer if available.

question_text: A 95% confidence interval for \(p\) is \([0.318,0.382]\). Without recomputing a test statistic, what would a matching two-sided level \(0.05\) test conclude for \(H_0:p=0.40\)? What about \(H_0:p=0.35\)?
solution_text: For the matching two-sided test, null values outside the 95% confidence interval are rejected at level \(0.05\), while values inside are not rejected. \(0.40\) is outside \([0.318,0.382]\), so reject \(H_0:p=0.40\). The value \(0.35\) is inside the interval, so do not reject \(H_0:p=0.35\).

For the matching two-sided test, null values outside the 95% confidence interval are rejected at level \(0.05\), while values inside are not rejected. \(0.40\) is outside \([0.318,0.382]\), so reject \(H_0:p=0.40\). The value \(0.35\) is inside the interval, so do not reject \(H_0:p=0.35\).

This shortcut does not directly apply to one-sided tests or to intervals built by a different method than the test.
thought_text: This shortcut does not directly apply to one-sided tests or to intervals built by a different method than the test.

question_text: A 95% confidence interval for a proportion based on \(n=625\) has \(\hat p=0.64\). What is its margin of error?
solution_text: The standard error is \[\sqrt{\frac{0.64(0.36)}{625}}=0.0192.\] For 95% confidence, the margin is \[E=1.96(0.0192)=0.0376.\] The interval would be \(0.64\pm0.0376\).

The standard error is \[\sqrt{\frac{0.64(0.36)}{625}}=0.0192.\] For 95% confidence, the margin is \[E=1.96(0.0192)=0.0376.\] The interval would be \(0.64\pm0.0376\).

The margin of error is the half-width of the interval, not the full width.
thought_text: The margin of error is the half-width of the interval, not the full width.

question_text: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(100,0.30)\). Use the normal approximation with continuity correction to estimate \(P(X\le25)\).
solution_text: The binomial mean is \(np=30\), and the standard deviation is \(\sqrt{np(1-p)}=\sqrt{21}=4.583\). The event \(X\le25\) is approximated by \(Y\le25.5\) for \(Y\sim N(30,21)\). \[z=\frac{25.5-30}{4.583}=-0.982.\] Thus \(P(X\le25)\approx P(Z\le-0.982)\approx0.163.\)
text_for_embedding:
Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(100,0.30)\). Use the normal approximation with continuity correction to estimate \(P(X\le25)\).

The binomial mean is \(np=30\), and the standard deviation is \(\sqrt{np(1-p)}=\sqrt{21}=4.583\). The event \(X\le25\) is approximated by \(Y\le25.5\) for \(Y\sim N(30,21)\). \[z=\frac{25.5-30}{4.583}=-0.982.\] Thus \(P(X\le25)\approx P(Z\le-0.982)\approx0.163.\)

Continuity correction is used when approximating the integer count (X), not usually when building the standard large-sample interval for (p).

question_text: A level \(0.05\) lower-tailed test of \(H_0:p=0.60\) is based on \(n=500\). Find the rejection rule in terms of \(\hat p\) using the normal approximation.
text_for_embedding:
A level \(0.05\) lower-tailed test of \(H_0:p=0.60\) is based on \(n=500\). Find the rejection rule in terms of \(\hat p\) using the normal approximation.

The lower-tail critical value is \(-z_{0.05}=-1.645\). Under \(H_0\), \[\text{SE}_0=\sqrt{\frac{0.60(0.40)}{500}}=0.02191.\] Reject when \[\frac{\hat p-0.60}{0.02191}<-1.645,\] or \[\hat p<0.60-1.645(0.02191)=0.564.\]

A rejection region can be written on the (z)-scale or converted back to the sample-proportion scale.
thought_text: A rejection region can be written on the (z)-scale or converted back to the sample-proportion scale.

question_text: A team chooses \(n=385\) using the conservative 95% design for margin about \(0.05\). Verify the worst-case margin of error when \(p=0.5\).
solution_text: The worst-case standard error is \(\sqrt{0.25/385}=0.02548\). The 95% worst-case margin is \[E=1.96(0.02548)=0.0499.\] Thus \(n=385\) achieves a margin just below \(0.05\) in the conservative worst case.

This common survey number comes from (1.96^2(0.25)/0.05^2=384.16), rounded up.

question_text: A student tests \(H_0:p=0.25\) using \(n=300\), \(x=90\), and writes \(z=(0.30-0.25)/\sqrt{0.30(0.70)/300}\). Identify the mistake and compute the correct test statistic.
solution_text: The student used the confidence-interval standard error based on \(\hat p=0.30\). For testing \(H_0:p=0.25\), the null standard error must use \(p_0=0.25\). \[\text{SE}_0=\sqrt{\frac{0.25(0.75)}{300}}=0.0250,\qquad z=\frac{0.30-0.25}{0.0250}=2.00.\]

question_text: A poll samples \(n=2000\) responses from an online forum where users can answer multiple times. The sample has many successes and failures. Is the usual one-proportion interval automatically justified? Explain.
solution_text: No. The success-failure condition is only one part of the justification. The data should also come from a random or otherwise representative sample, and the observations should be approximately independent. If users can answer multiple times, responses may be duplicated or dependent and may not represent the target population. A large count of successes and failures does not fix a biased or dependent sampling design.

A large count of successes and failures does not fix a biased or dependent sampling design.

Statistical formulas cannot repair a poor data-collection process. Always separate design assumptions from normal-approximation conditions.

question_text: In a two-sided test for a proportion, the observed statistic is \(z=-2.18\). Approximate the P-value and state the decision at \(\alpha=0.01\).
solution_text: For a two-sided test, \[P\text{-value}=2P(Z\le-2.18).\] Since \(P(Z\le-2.18)\approx0.0146\), the P-value is about \(0.0292\). Because \(0.0292>0.01\), do not reject \(H_0\) at the 1% level.

question_text: A symmetric large-sample confidence interval for \(p\) is reported as \([0.212,0.288]\). What are \(\hat p\) and the margin of error?
solution_text: The point estimate is the midpoint: \[\hat p=\frac{0.212+0.288}{2}=0.250.\] The margin of error is the half-width: \[E=\frac{0.288-0.212}{2}=0.038.\]

The sample proportion is at the center of the usual Wald interval.
thought_text: The sample proportion is at the center of the usual Wald interval.

question_text: A student writes: "In a sample of \(n=50\), \(x=3\). A 95% confidence interval is \(0.06\pm1.96\sqrt{0.06(0.94)/50}=[-0.006,0.126]\), so the true proportion might be negative." Identify the statistical issues and give an appropriate conclusion for this course.
solution_text: The arithmetic follows the usual Wald formula, but the method is not well justified: \(n\hat p=3<10\). The lower endpoint is negative, which is impossible for a population proportion and is another warning that the normal approximation is poor here. An appropriate conclusion is that the usual large-sample confidence interval should not be trusted for these data. One should use an exact binomial or adjusted interval if available; at minimum, report that the data suggest a small positive proportion but the standard large-sample interval is invalid or unreliable.

question_text: Derive the usual large-sample two-sided confidence interval for \(p\) from the approximate normal distribution of \(\hat p\).
solution_text: For a binomial sample, \(\hat p=X/n\) has mean \(p\) and variance \(p(1-p)/n\). For large samples, \[\frac{\hat p-p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}}\approx N(0,1).\] Because \(p\) is unknown, the standard error is estimated by replacing \(p\) with \(\hat p\), giving \[\widehat{\text{SE}}=\sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}}.\] Thus an approximate \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) confidence interval is \[\hat p\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}}.\]
text_for_embedding:
Derive the usual large-sample two-sided confidence interval for \(p\) from the approximate normal distribution of \(\hat p\).

For a binomial sample, \(\hat p=X/n\) has mean \(p\) and variance \(p(1-p)/n\). For large samples, \[\frac{\hat p-p}{\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}}\approx N(0,1).\] Because \(p\) is unknown, the standard error is estimated by replacing \(p\) with \(\hat p\), giving \[\widehat{\text{SE}}=\sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}}.\] Thus an approximate \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) confidence interval is \[\hat p\pm z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{\hat p(1-\hat p)}{n}}.\]

This derivation explains why the CI standard error and the test standard error are not the same expression.
thought_text: This derivation explains why the CI standard error and the test standard error are not the same expression.

question_text: A level \(0.05\) upper-tailed test of \(H_0:p=0.40\) uses \(n=200\). Approximate the probability of rejecting \(H_0\) if the true proportion is \(p=0.50\).
solution_text: First find the rejection cutoff under \(H_0\). The null standard error is \(\sqrt{0.4(0.6)/200}=0.03464\). Reject for \[\hat p>0.40+1.645(0.03464)=0.4570.\] If the true \(p=0.50\), then \(\hat p\approx N(0.50,0.50(0.50)/200)\), with standard deviation \(0.03536\). Therefore \[P(\text{reject}\mid p=0.50)\approx P\left(\hat p>0.4570\right)=P\left(Z>\frac{0.4570-0.5000}{0.03536}\right).\] The \(z\)-value is \(-1.22\), so the probability is \(P(Z>-1.22)\approx0.889\).

Therefore \[P(\text{reject}\mid p=0.50)\approx P\left(\hat p>0.4570\right)=P\left(Z>\frac{0.4570-0.5000}{0.03536}\right).\] The \(z\)-value is \(-1.22\), so the probability is \(P(Z>-1.22)\approx0.889\).

question_text: A two-sided test of \(H_0:p=0.10\) uses \(n=120\) and observes \(x=20\). Compute the usual large-sample \(z\)-test P-value. Then approximate the exact binomial tail using a continuity-corrected normal approximation to \(X\), and compare.
solution_text: The usual test uses \(\hat p=20/120=0.1667\) and null standard error \(\sqrt{0.10(0.90)/120}=0.02739\). Thus \[z=\frac{0.1667-0.1000}{0.02739}=2.43.\] The two-sided \(z\)-test P-value is about \(2P(Z\ge2.43)=0.015.\) On the count scale under \(H_0\), \(X\approx N(12,10.8)\). Since the observed count is above the null mean, use \(P(X\ge20)\approx P(Y\ge19.5)\). The corrected statistic is \[(19.5-12)/\sqrt{10.8}=2.28.\] Doubling for a two-sided approximation gives about \(2P(Z\ge2.28)=0.023.\) The two answers are close in conclusion but not identical. Continuity correction on the count scale can matter when counts are not very large.

question_text: You can afford \(n=1000\) observations and want a conservative margin of error at most \(0.025\) for a proportion. What is the largest two-sided confidence level you can guarantee using \(p^*=0.5\)?
solution_text: The conservative margin is \[E=z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{\frac{0.25}{1000}}=z_{\alpha/2}(0.01581).\] Requiring \(E\le0.025\) gives \(z_{\alpha/2}\le0.025/0.01581=1.581\). The largest confidence level uses \(z=1.581\), so \[1-\alpha=P(-1.581\le Z\le1.581)=2\Phi(1.581)-1.\] Since \(\Phi(1.58)\approx0.943\), the confidence level is about \(2(0.943)-1=0.886\), or \(88.6\%\).

question_text: A report says: "The test of \(H_0:p=0.50\) gave \(P\text{-value}=0.07\), so there is a 7% chance that exactly half the population supports the policy. Because 7% is small, we have proved \(p\ne0.50\)." Diagnose the errors.
solution_text: The P-value is not the probability that \(H_0\) is true. It is the probability, assuming \(p=0.50\), of observing a statistic at least as extreme as the one obtained. Also, whether \(0.07\) is small enough depends on the prechosen significance level. At \(\alpha=0.05\), \(0.07>0.05\), so the test would not reject \(H_0\). Even if a test rejects, it does not prove the alternative; it gives statistical evidence against the null under the model assumptions.

question_text: For each situation, choose the correct method or explain why the usual method is not justified. Do not compute final numeric endpoints unless needed. \(n=640\), \(x=352\), estimate \(p\) with 95% confidence. \(n=90\), \(x=2\), estimate \(p\) with 95% confidence. \(n=500\), test \(H_0:p=0.30\) against \(H_1:p<0.30\). \(n=60\), test \(H_0:p=0.02\) using a large-sample \(z\)-test.
solution_text: (i) The usual large-sample one-proportion confidence interval is appropriate: \[\hat p\pm1.96\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)/640},\quad \hat p=352/640.\] (ii) The usual large-sample interval is not justified because \(n\hat p=2<10\). Use an exact or adjusted method if permitted. (iii) Use the one-proportion \(z\)-test with \[z=\frac{\hat p-0.30}{\sqrt{0.30(0.70)/500}}\] and a lower-tail P-value. (iv) The normal approximation under the null is not justified because \(np_0=60(0.02)=1.2<10\). Do not rely on the usual large-sample \(z\)-test.

question_text: A rare-event study observes \(x=0\) events in \(n=75\) trials. A student reports the large-sample interval \(0\pm1.96\sqrt{0(1)/75}=[0,0]\) and concludes the event is impossible. Diagnose the solution.
solution_text: The solution is invalid. The success-failure condition fails completely because \(n\hat p=0\). The estimated standard error is zero only because the sample observed no events; it does not prove the true population proportion is zero. The conclusion that the event is impossible is unjustified. A rare event may simply not appear in a small sample. An exact binomial upper confidence bound or another rare-event method should be used if the goal is to bound \(p\).

question_text: Two independent production lines are monitored for defects. Line A has \(18\) defective parts among \(150\), and Line B has \(31\) defective parts among \(170\). Test \(H_0:p_A-p_B=0\) against \(H_1:p_A-p_B\ne0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\) using the large-sample two-proportion \(z\)-test.
solution_text: The sample proportions are \(\hat p_A=18/150=0.120\) and \(\hat p_B=31/170=0.182\). Under \(H_0:p_A=p_B\), use the pooled estimate \[\hat p=\frac{18+31}{150+170}=\frac{49}{320}=0.1531.\] The null standard error is \[\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)\left(\frac1{150}+\frac1{170}\right)}=\sqrt{0.1531(0.8469)(0.01255)}=0.0403.\] Thus \[z=\frac{0.120-0.182}{0.0403}=-1.54.\] The two-sided P-value is about \(2P(Z\le-1.54)=0.124\). Since \(0.124>0.05\), do not reject \(H_0\). The data do not give significant evidence that the two defect proportions differ.

The sample proportions are \(\hat p_A=18/150=0.120\) and \(\hat p_B=31/170=0.182\). Under \(H_0:p_A=p_B\), use the pooled estimate \[\hat p=\frac{18+31}{150+170}=\frac{49}{320}=0.1531.\] The null standard error is \[\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)\left(\frac1{150}+\frac1{170}\right)}=\sqrt{0.1531(0.8469)(0.01255)}=0.0403.\] Thus \[z=\frac{0.120-0.182}{0.0403}=-1.54.\] The two-sided P-value is about \(2P(Z\le-1.54)=0.124\). Since \(0.124>0.05\), do not reject \(H_0\). The data do not give significant evidence that the two defect proportions differ.

For a two-proportion test of equality, the standard error uses the pooled proportion; confidence intervals use the unpooled standard error.

question_text: For each setting, choose one-proportion inference, two-proportion inference, or explain why the usual large-sample method is not justified. Do not compute final numeric answers. One sample has \(x=42\) successes in \(n=300\), and the null claim is \(p=0.10\). Two independent samples have \(x_1=64,n_1=400\) and \(x_2=51,n_2=420\), and the question asks whether \(p_1-p_2=0\). Two independent samples have \(x_1=2,n_1=80\) and \(x_2=9,n_2=90\), and the question asks whether their proportions differ.
solution_text: (i) This is one-proportion inference because there is one binomial sample and one null value \(p_0=0.10\). Use \[z=\frac{\hat p-p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}}\] if the null success-failure counts are adequate. (ii) This is two-proportion inference with independent samples. For a test of \(H_0:p_1-p_2=0\), use the pooled proportion and the two-proportion \(z\)-statistic. (iii) The intended method is two-proportion inference, but the usual large-sample \(z\)-test is questionable because one sample has only \(x_1=2\) successes. An exact or alternative method would be preferable if available.

Two independent samples have \(x_1=2,n_1=80\) and \(x_2=9,n_2=90\), and the question asks whether their proportions differ.

(i) This is one-proportion inference because there is one binomial sample and one null value \(p_0=0.10\). For a test of \(H_0:p_1-p_2=0\), use the pooled proportion and the two-proportion \(z\)-statistic. (iii) The intended method is two-proportion inference, but the usual large-sample \(z\)-test is questionable because one sample has only \(x_1=2\) successes. An exact or alternative method would be preferable if available.

question_text: A right-tail F table gives \(f_{0.05,8,5}=4.82\), where \(P(F_{8,5}>f_{0.05,8,5})=0.05\). Use the reciprocal identity to find \(f_{0.95,5,8}\).
solution_text:
The reciprocal identity is \[f_{1-\alpha,
u_1,
u_2}= rac{1}{f_{\alpha,
u_2,
u_1}}.\] Thus \[f_{0.95,5,8}= rac{1}{f_{0.05,8,5}}= rac{1}{4.82}=0.207.\] This is a left-tail cutoff: \(P(F_{5,8}<0.207)=0.05\).

A right-tail F table gives \(f_{0.05,8,5}=4.82\), where \(P(F_{8,5}>f_{0.05,8,5})=0.05\). Use the reciprocal identity to find \(f_{0.95,5,8}\).

The reciprocal identity is \[f_{1-\alpha,\nu_1,\nu_2}=\frac{1}{f_{\alpha,\nu_2,\nu_1}}.\] Thus \[f_{0.95,5,8}=\frac{1}{f_{0.05,8,5}}=\frac{1}{4.82}=0.207.\] This is a left-tail cutoff: \(P(F_{5,8}<0.207)=0.05\).

Two things change together: the tail probability and the order of the degrees of freedom.
thought_text: Two things change together: the tail probability and the order of the degrees of freedom.

question_text: Independent normal samples from process A and process B give \(n_1=15\), \(s_1^2=5.6\), \(n_2=15\), and \(s_2^2=2.1\). At \(\alpha=0.05\), test \(H_0:\sigma_1^2\le\sigma_2^2\) against \(H_1:\sigma_1^2>\sigma_2^2\). Use \(f_{0.05,14,14}=2.48\).
solution_text: Use \[F=\frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2}=\frac{5.6}{2.1}=2.67,\qquad F\sim F_{14,14}\text{ under }H_0\text{ at equality}.\] The rejection region is \(F>f_{0.05,14,14}=2.48\). Since \(2.67>2.48\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence at the 5% level that process A has larger variance.

A one-sided test uses the full (alpha) in one tail. Do not split it.

solution_text: With \(S_1^2/S_2^2\) as written, \[F=\frac{8.4}{3.1}=2.71,\qquad F\sim F_{9,11}\text{ under }H_0.\] For a two-sided test, reject if \(S_1^2/S_2^2>3.59\) or if \(S_2^2/S_1^2>3.91\). Here \(2.71<3.59\), and the reciprocal \(3.1/8.4=0.369<3.91\). Do not reject \(H_0\).

Two independent normal samples have \(n_1=10\), \(s_1^2=8.4\), \(n_2=12\), and \(s_2^2=3.1\). Test \(H_0:\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2\) against \(H_1:\sigma_1^2\ne\sigma_2^2\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(f_{0.025,9,11}=3.59\) and \(f_{0.025,11,9}=3.91\).

With \(S_1^2/S_2^2\) as written, \[F=\frac{8.4}{3.1}=2.71,\qquad F\sim F_{9,11}\text{ under }H_0.\] For a two-sided test, reject if \(S_1^2/S_2^2>3.59\) or if \(S_2^2/S_1^2>3.91\). Here \(2.71<3.59\), and the reciprocal \(3.1/8.4=0.369<3.91\). Do not reject \(H_0\).

For two-sided F tests, the lower tail is usually handled by flipping the ratio and swapping the degrees of freedom.
thought_text: For two-sided F tests, the lower tail is usually handled by flipping the ratio and swapping the degrees of freedom.

question_text: Independent normal samples give \(n_1=9\), \(s_1^2=4.0\), \(n_2=11\), \(s_2^2=10.0\). Test \(H_0:\sigma_1^2\ge\sigma_2^2\) against \(H_1:\sigma_1^2<\sigma_2^2\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(f_{0.05,10,8}=3.35\).
solution_text: If we keep \(F=S_1^2/S_2^2\), then \(F=0.40\) and the rejection region is a left tail. Instead use the reciprocal: \[\frac{S_2^2}{S_1^2}=\frac{10.0}{4.0}=2.50\sim F_{10,8}\text{ under equality}.\] Reject for \(S_2^2/S_1^2>f_{0.05,10,8}=3.35\). Since \(2.50<3.35\), do not reject \(H_0\).

question_text: A statistician computes \(F=S_A^2/S_B^2\) from independent normal samples with \(n_A=7\) and \(n_B=13\). Under \(H_0:\sigma_A^2=\sigma_B^2\), what is the null distribution of \(F\)?
solution_text: Since sample variance \(S_A^2\) is based on \(n_A-1=6\) degrees of freedom and appears in the numerator, and \(S_B^2\) is based on \(n_B-1=12\) degrees of freedom and appears in the denominator, \[F=\frac{S_A^2}{S_B^2}\sim F_{6,12}\] under \(H_0\).

question_text: List the main assumptions needed for the classical F-test comparing two variances. Which assumption is usually the most fragile?
solution_text: The classical F-test assumes independent random samples, independence between the two samples, and normality of both populations. The normality assumption is usually the most fragile because the F-test is sensitive to non-normality, especially skewness and heavy tails. The test is exact under normality; without normality, its nominal level can be badly wrong.

The normality assumption is usually the most fragile because the F-test is sensitive to non-normality, especially skewness and heavy tails. The test is exact under normality; without normality, its nominal level can be badly wrong.

Variance tests are much less robust to non-normality than large-sample mean tests.
thought_text: Variance tests are much less robust to non-normality than large-sample mean tests.

question_text: For independent normal samples, write the two-sided \(100(1-\alpha)\%\) confidence interval for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\) in terms of \(s_1^2/s_2^2\), \(n_1\), and \(n_2\). Use the course convention that \(f_{\gamma,\nu_1,\nu_2}\) is a right-tail critical value.
solution_text: The pivot is \[\frac{S_1^2/S_2^2}{\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2}\sim F_{n_1-1,n_2-1}.\] Therefore \[\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2}\in\left[\frac{s_1^2/s_2^2}{f_{\alpha/2,n_1-1,n_2-1}},\ \frac{s_1^2/s_2^2}{f_{1-\alpha/2,n_1-1,n_2-1}}\right].\] Equivalently, use the reciprocal identity for the upper endpoint: \((s_1^2/s_2^2)f_{\alpha/2,n_2-1,n_1-1}\).

The unknown ratio appears in the denominator of the pivot, so the endpoint order comes from solving inequalities carefully.

question_text: Explain the meaning of \(f_{0.025,6,9}=5.80\). What probability statement does it represent?
solution_text: It means \[P(F_{6,9}>5.80)=0.025.\] Equivalently, \(P(F_{6,9}\le 5.80)=0.975\). The subscript \(0.025\) is the right-tail area, not the confidence level.

This notation is the same style as (z_alpha) and (t_{alpha, u}) in earlier chapters.

question_text: In a two-sided F-test, \(F_{\text{obs}}=4.10\) for \(F_{8,8}\), and the upper-tail probability is \(P(F_{8,8}\ge4.10)=0.032\). What is the approximate two-sided P-value if the observed ratio is above 1?
solution_text: For a two-sided F-test with the observed statistic in the upper tail, the approximate two-sided P-value is twice the one-sided upper-tail probability: \[p\approx2(0.032)=0.064.\] This doubling is appropriate because the other extreme direction corresponds to the reciprocal tail.

question_text: Independent normal samples give \(n_1=12\), \(s_1^2=18.0\), \(n_2=10\), and \(s_2^2=9.0\). Construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\). Use \(f_{0.025,11,9}=3.91\) and \(f_{0.025,9,11}=3.59\).
text_for_embedding:
Independent normal samples give \(n_1=12\), \(s_1^2=18.0\), \(n_2=10\), and \(s_2^2=9.0\). Construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\). Use \(f_{0.025,11,9}=3.91\) and \(f_{0.025,9,11}=3.59\).

The sample variance ratio is \(s_1^2/s_2^2=18/9=2.00\). Using the reciprocal form, \[\left[\frac{2.00}{f_{0.025,11,9}},\ 2.00 f_{0.025,9,11}\right]=\left[\frac{2.00}{3.91},\ 2.00(3.59)\right]=[0.511,\ 7.18].\]

A CI for a variance ratio can be very wide with small samples; that is expected, not a calculation failure.
thought_text: A CI for a variance ratio can be very wide with small samples; that is expected, not a calculation failure.

question_text: A researcher computes \(s_A^2=20.0\) from \(n_A=13\) and \(s_B^2=8.0\) from \(n_B=9\). Test \(H_0:\sigma_A^2\le\sigma_B^2\) against \(H_1:\sigma_A^2>\sigma_B^2\) at \(\alpha=0.01\). Use \(f_{0.01,12,8}=5.67\).
solution_text: The statistic matching the alternative is \[F=\frac{S_A^2}{S_B^2}=\frac{20.0}{8.0}=2.50,\qquad F\sim F_{12,8}\text{ under equality}.\] Reject for \(F>5.67\). Since \(2.50<5.67\), do not reject. Although the sample variance for A is larger, it is not large enough for a 1% one-sided conclusion.

question_text: For the data in Q12, suppose another student uses \(F'=S_B^2/S_A^2=0.40\). Explain how this can produce the same decision if the degrees of freedom and tail are handled correctly.
solution_text: Using \(F'=S_B^2/S_A^2\) gives \(F'\sim F_{8,12}\) under equality. The alternative \(\sigma_A^2>\sigma_B^2\) is now a lower-tail alternative because \(S_B^2/S_A^2\) should be small. The lower-tail cutoff is \(f_{0.99,8,12}=1/f_{0.01,12,8}=1/5.67=0.176\). Reject if \(F'0.176\), do not reject, matching Q12.

The lower-tail cutoff is \(f_{0.99,8,12}=1/f_{0.01,12,8}=1/5.67=0.176\). Reject if \(F'0.176\), do not reject, matching Q12.

question_text: A 95% confidence interval for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\) is \([0.62,2.84]\). What would the matching two-sided 5% F-test of \(H_0:\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2\) conclude? What wording should be avoided?
solution_text: The null value for equal variances is \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2=1\). Since 1 lies inside \([0.62,2.84]\), the matching two-sided 5% F-test would fail to reject \(H_0\). Avoid saying "the variances are equal" or "we proved equality." The correct conclusion is that the data do not provide statistically significant evidence of unequal variances at the 5% level.

Failing to reject equal variances is not evidence strong enough to establish equal variances.
thought_text: Failing to reject equal variances is not evidence strong enough to establish equal variances.

question_text: Two samples of waiting times are highly right-skewed. A student applies the classical F-test for equality of variances because both sample sizes exceed 40. Diagnose the reasoning.
solution_text: The reasoning is unsafe. The exact F-test for variance equality is a normal-sample result. Large sample sizes do not automatically make the sample variance ratio have an F distribution when the population is strongly non-normal. For skewed waiting times, a transformation, a model appropriate for waiting times, Levene/Brown-Forsythe style procedures, or a bootstrap/permutation method may be more appropriate, depending on course permissions.

For skewed waiting times, a transformation, a model appropriate for waiting times, Levene/Brown-Forsythe style procedures, or a bootstrap/permutation method may be more appropriate, depending on course permissions.

The CLT helps the sample mean. It does not make (S_1^2/S_2^2) exactly F-distributed.
thought_text: The CLT helps the sample mean. It does not make (S_1^2/S_2^2) exactly F-distributed.

question_text: An analyst says: "First I will run an F-test at 5% for equal variances. If it does not reject, I will use the pooled two-sample T-test; otherwise I will use Welch's T-test." Explain the caveat and the safer default.
solution_text: This pre-test strategy uses the same data to choose the subsequent test, so the nominal error rate of the whole procedure is not as simple as the advertised 5%. Also, the F-test has low power against many variance alternatives and is sensitive to non-normality. If the F-test is used only as a screen for whether pooled T is safe, the concerning error is often Type II error: failing to detect unequal variances and then using pooled T. That is why some course notes recommend a larger screening \(\alpha\). The safer modern default is to skip the variance pre-test and use Welch's T-test unless equal variances are justified by design or substantive knowledge.

That is why some course notes recommend a larger screening \(\alpha\). The safer modern default is to skip the variance pre-test and use Welch's T-test unless equal variances are justified by design or substantive knowledge.

A non-significant F-test is a weak reason to pool variances.
thought_text: A non-significant F-test is a weak reason to pool variances.

question_text: Two normal samples have \(n_1=n_2=10\), \(s_1^2=7.8\), and \(s_2^2=4.6\). Use a two-sided F screening test at \(\alpha=0.10\) for equality of variances before a pooled T-test. Use \(f_{0.05,9,9}=3.18\).
solution_text: The observed ratio is \(F=7.8/4.6=1.70\). For a two-sided 10% test with equal sample sizes, reject if \(F>3.18\) or \(F<1/3.18=0.314\). Since \(1.70\) is inside the acceptance region, do not reject equality of variances. If following the course screening convention, this would permit the pooled T-test, but the caveat remains that Welch is generally safer than pre-test selection.

question_text: An OC curve for a two-sided F-test plots \(P(\text{accept }H_0)\) on the vertical axis against \(\lambda=\sigma_1/\sigma_2\) on the horizontal axis. Interpret the point \(\lambda=1.5\), \(P(\text{accept }H_0)=0.42\). What is the power at this variance-ratio alternative?
solution_text: The value \(\lambda=1.5\) means the true standard deviation in population 1 is 1.5 times that in population 2, so the true variance ratio is \(\lambda^2=2.25\). If the OC value is 0.42, then under this alternative the test fails to reject equal variances 42% of the time. The power is \[1-0.42=0.58.\]

The value \(\lambda=1.5\) means the true standard deviation in population 1 is 1.5 times that in population 2, so the true variance ratio is \(\lambda^2=2.25\). If the OC value is 0.42, then under this alternative the test fails to reject equal variances 42% of the time. The power is \[1-0.42=0.58.\]

OC curves show Type II behavior; power is one minus the OC value.
thought_text: OC curves show Type II behavior; power is one minus the OC value.

question_text: Why can a small-sample F-test fail to reject equal variances even when the sample variances differ by a factor of 2? Answer in terms of power and the F distribution.
solution_text: With small degrees of freedom, the F distribution has heavy right tails and wide variability. Therefore the critical values for a two-sided F-test are far from 1, and a sample variance ratio of 2 may be ordinary under \(H_0\). Equivalently, the test has low power for moderate alternatives. Failing to reject can simply mean the sample size was too small to detect a practically meaningful variance difference.

Therefore the critical values for a two-sided F-test are far from 1, and a sample variance ratio of 2 may be ordinary under \(H_0\). Equivalently, the test has low power for moderate alternatives. Failing to reject can simply mean the sample size was too small to detect a practically meaningful variance difference.

A factor of 2 in sample variances sounds large, but small-sample F critical values can be larger still.
thought_text: A factor of 2 in sample variances sounds large, but small-sample F critical values can be larger still.

question_text: Independent normal samples give \(n_1=16\), \(s_1^2=12.0\), \(n_2=16\), and \(s_2^2=8.0\). Find a 95% upper confidence bound for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\). Use \(f_{0.05,15,15}=2.40\).
solution_text: The ratio estimate is \(s_1^2/s_2^2=1.5\). For a one-sided upper bound, solve \[P\left(\frac{S_1^2/S_2^2}{\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2}>f_{0.95,15,15}\right)=0.95\] or use the reciprocal form. With equal degrees of freedom, \(1/f_{0.95,15,15}=f_{0.05,15,15}=2.40\). Thus the 95% upper bound is \[\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2}\le (1.5)(2.40)=3.60.\]
text_for_embedding:
Independent normal samples give \(n_1=16\), \(s_1^2=12.0\), \(n_2=16\), and \(s_2^2=8.0\). Find a 95% upper confidence bound for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\). Use \(f_{0.05,15,15}=2.40\).

The ratio estimate is \(s_1^2/s_2^2=1.5\). For a one-sided upper bound, solve \[P\left(\frac{S_1^2/S_2^2}{\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2}>f_{0.95,15,15}\right)=0.95\] or use the reciprocal form. With equal degrees of freedom, \(1/f_{0.95,15,15}=f_{0.05,15,15}=2.40\). Thus the 95% upper bound is \[\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2}\le (1.5)(2.40)=3.60.\]

A one-sided upper confidence bound for the ratio multiplies by the right-tail critical value with swapped degrees of freedom.
thought_text: A one-sided upper confidence bound for the ratio multiplies by the right-tail critical value with swapped degrees of freedom.

question_text: For independent normal samples, a 90% confidence interval for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\) is \([1.12,5.80]\). What does the matching two-sided \(\alpha=0.10\) F-test of equal variances conclude? What if the test is at \(\alpha=0.05\)?
solution_text: For the matching two-sided 10% test, reject \(H_0:\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2=1\) because 1 is outside the 90% confidence interval. For a 5% test, this 90% interval alone is not enough to decide. The matching interval would be a 95% confidence interval, which is wider and might include 1. We need the 95% interval or the test statistic and 5% critical values.

question_text: Sample 1 is recorded in centimeters and sample 2 is recorded in meters by mistake. A student directly computes \(S_1^2/S_2^2\) and performs an F-test. What is wrong, and how should the data be handled?
solution_text: Variances scale with the square of the measurement unit. If sample 1 is in centimeters and sample 2 is in meters, the raw variance ratio contains a factor of \(100^2=10{,}000\) due only to units. Both samples must be converted to the same units before computing sample variances and the F statistic. After unit conversion, the F-test compares physical variability rather than measurement-scale artifacts.

Both samples must be converted to the same units before computing sample variances and the F statistic. After unit conversion, the F-test compares physical variability rather than measurement-scale artifacts.

Variance ratios are unitless only after both variances are expressed in the same unit.
thought_text: Variance ratios are unitless only after both variances are expressed in the same unit.

question_text: Derive the pivot used to compare \(\sigma_1^2\) and \(\sigma_2^2\) from two independent normal samples.
solution_text: For independent normal samples, \[(n_1-1)\frac{S_1^2}{\sigma_1^2}\sim\chi^2_{n_1-1},\qquad (n_2-1)\frac{S_2^2}{\sigma_2^2}\sim\chi^2_{n_2-1},\] and these two chi-squared variables are independent. Taking the ratio after dividing by degrees of freedom gives \[\frac{\left((n_1-1)S_1^2/\sigma_1^2\right)/(n_1-1)}{\left((n_2-1)S_2^2/\sigma_2^2\right)/(n_2-1)}=\frac{S_1^2/S_2^2}{\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2}\sim F_{n_1-1,n_2-1}.\]
text_for_embedding:
Derive the pivot used to compare \(\sigma_1^2\) and \(\sigma_2^2\) from two independent normal samples.

For independent normal samples, \[(n_1-1)\frac{S_1^2}{\sigma_1^2}\sim\chi^2_{n_1-1},\qquad (n_2-1)\frac{S_2^2}{\sigma_2^2}\sim\chi^2_{n_2-1},\] and these two chi-squared variables are independent. Taking the ratio after dividing by degrees of freedom gives \[\frac{\left((n_1-1)S_1^2/\sigma_1^2\right)/(n_1-1)}{\left((n_2-1)S_2^2/\sigma_2^2\right)/(n_2-1)}=\frac{S_1^2/S_2^2}{\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2}\sim F_{n_1-1,n_2-1}.\]

This derivation is the reason the F-test is exact only under normality and independence.
thought_text: This derivation is the reason the F-test is exact only under normality and independence.

question_text: A student writes: "Since \(s_1^2=25\), \(s_2^2=9\), \(n_1=8\), \(n_2=12\), the 95% two-sided F-test uses \(F=25/9=2.78\sim F_{8,12}\). Compare to \(f_{0.05,8,12}=3.28\). Since \(2.78<3.28\), do not reject." Identify at least three errors and give the corrected test setup. Use \(f_{0.025,7,11}=4.71\) and \(f_{0.025,11,7}=5.22\).
solution_text: Errors: the degrees of freedom should be \(7\) and \(11\), not \(8\) and \(12\); a two-sided 5% test uses \(\alpha/2=0.025\) in each tail; and the lower tail must be checked by the reciprocal ratio or a left-tail cutoff. It is also incomplete because the normality and independence assumptions are not stated. Correct setup: \(F=S_1^2/S_2^2=25/9=2.78\sim F_{7,11}\) under \(H_0\). Reject if \(S_1^2/S_2^2>f_{0.025,7,11}=4.71\) or if \(S_2^2/S_1^2>f_{0.025,11,7}=5.22\). Since \(2.78<4.71\) and \(9/25=0.36<5.22\), do not reject.

question_text: A report says: "The F-test for equal variances has \(p=0.18>0.05\), so the variances are equal. Therefore the pooled T-test is definitely valid." Diagnose the statement.
solution_text: The report overstates the conclusion. \(p=0.18\) means the F-test did not find significant evidence of unequal variances; it does not prove equality. The test may have low power, especially with small samples or moderate variance differences. The word "definitely" is also unjustified because the F-test depends on normality and independence. As a workflow issue, using an F pre-test to choose between pooled and Welch can distort the overall analysis. A safer default is Welch's T-test unless equal variances are justified externally.
text_for_embedding:
A report says: "The F-test for equal variances has \(p=0.18>0.05\), so the variances are equal. Therefore the pooled T-test is definitely valid." Diagnose the statement.

The report overstates the conclusion. \(p=0.18\) means the F-test did not find significant evidence of unequal variances; it does not prove equality. The test may have low power, especially with small samples or moderate variance differences. The word "definitely" is also unjustified because the F-test depends on normality and independence. As a workflow issue, using an F pre-test to choose between pooled and Welch can distort the overall analysis. A safer default is Welch's T-test unless equal variances are justified externally.

Non-rejection is not validation; it is only lack of evidence against the null under a model.
thought_text: Non-rejection is not validation; it is only lack of evidence against the null under a model.

question_text: A quality team wants a two-sided F-test at \(\alpha=0.05\) to have at least 80% power when \(\sigma_1/\sigma_2=1.6\). Their current design has \(n_1=n_2=8\), and the OC curve gives \(P(\text{accept }H_0)=0.55\) at \(\lambda=1.6\). Is the design adequate? What should change?
solution_text: The current power is \(1-0.55=0.45\), far below the desired 0.80. The design is not adequate for detecting \(\sigma_1/\sigma_2=1.6\) reliably. The team should increase sample sizes, reduce measurement noise if possible, or accept lower power. Merely changing the observed decision rule after seeing the data would invalidate the planned level. Increasing \(\alpha\) would increase power but also increases Type I error, so it must be a planned tradeoff.

question_text: Two variances are computed from before-and-after measurements on the same 20 subjects. A student applies the two-sample F-test to compare the before variance with the after variance. What assumption is violated, and what is the consequence?
solution_text: The two-sample F-test assumes the two sample variances come from independent samples. Before-and-after measurements on the same subjects are paired, so the two sample variances are generally dependent. Because the independence condition behind the F pivot fails, \(S_{\text{before}}^2/S_{\text{after}}^2\) does not generally follow the stated \(F_{19,19}\) distribution. The reported critical values and P-values are therefore not valid without a method designed for dependent variances.

Before-and-after measurements on the same subjects are paired, so the two sample variances are generally dependent. Because the independence condition behind the F pivot fails, \(S_{\text{before}}^2/S_{\text{after}}^2\) does not generally follow the stated \(F_{19,19}\) distribution. The reported critical values and P-values are therefore not valid without a method designed for dependent variances.

Independence is part of the F pivot derivation, not a cosmetic assumption.
thought_text: Independence is part of the F pivot derivation, not a cosmetic assumption.

question_text: Independent normal samples have \(n_1=14\), \(s_1=6.0\), \(n_2=11\), and \(s_2=3.0\). Construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\sigma_1^2/\sigma_2^2\), then use it to test equality of variances at 5%. Use \(f_{0.025,13,10}=3.72\) and \(f_{0.025,10,13}=3.31\).
solution_text: The sample variance ratio is \[s_1^2/s_2^2=36/9=4.00.\] The 95% CI is \[\left[\frac{4.00}{3.72},\ 4.00(3.31)\right]=[1.08,\ 13.24].\] Since the null ratio 1 is below the interval, the matching two-sided 5% F-test rejects equality of variances. There is evidence that population 1 has larger variance.

question_text: For \(n_1=6\), \(n_2=20\), and a two-sided \(\alpha=0.05\) F-test using \(F=S_1^2/S_2^2\), specify both rejection-region cutoffs using only right-tail critical values. Do not compute table numbers.
solution_text: Here \(\nu_1=5\) and \(\nu_2=19\). The upper-tail rejection is \[S_1^2/S_2^2>f_{0.025,5,19}.\] The lower-tail rejection can be written with the reciprocal statistic: \[S_2^2/S_1^2>f_{0.025,19,5}.\] Equivalently, in terms of \(F=S_1^2/S_2^2\), the lower cutoff is \(F<1/f_{0.025,19,5}\).

question_text: A dataset has two independent groups with visible outliers in both groups. The scientific question is whether variability differs between groups. Should the analyst report the classical F-test as the main result? Give a statistically defensible answer.
solution_text: Probably not as the main result. Outliers and heavy tails directly affect sample variances and also violate the normality condition that makes the F-test exact. The resulting P-value may reflect tail behavior rather than a clean difference in normal-population variances. A defensible answer is to show diagnostics, consider transformations if scientifically meaningful, and use a more robust variance-comparison method such as Levene or Brown-Forsythe, or a resampling approach. If the classical F-test is still reported, it should be labeled as model-dependent and interpreted cautiously.

Probably not as the main result. Outliers and heavy tails directly affect sample variances and also violate the normality condition that makes the F-test exact. The resulting P-value may reflect tail behavior rather than a clean difference in normal-population variances. A defensible answer is to show diagnostics, consider transformations if scientifically meaningful, and use a more robust variance-comparison method such as Levene or Brown-Forsythe, or a resampling approach. If the classical F-test is still reported, it should be labeled as model-dependent and interpreted cautiously.

For variance questions, outliers are not a nuisance detail; they can be the entire result.
thought_text: For variance questions, outliers are not a nuisance detail; they can be the entire result.

question_text: A final-exam solution says: "The two samples are independent with \(n_1=18,n_2=10,s_1=4,s_2=7\). Because the larger sample standard deviation is in sample 2, put it on top: \(F=7/4=1.75\). Use \(F_{18,10}\), compare to \(f_{0.025,18,10}\), and since it is not significant, accept equal variances and use pooled T." Identify as many errors or caveats as you can.
solution_text: Major errors: the statistic must use variances, so the ratio with sample 2 on top is \(s_2^2/s_1^2=49/16=3.06\), not \(7/4\). The degrees of freedom are \(n_2-1=9\) over \(n_1-1=17\), so the null distribution for that ratio is \(F_{9,17}\), not \(F_{18,10}\). A two-sided test needs two tails or a reciprocal check, and the correct critical value would match the chosen numerator and denominator degrees of freedom. Caveats: the F-test also requires normality, not just independence. "Accept equal variances" is too strong; the correct wording is fail to reject equality. Finally, using an F pre-test to decide whether to use pooled T has the pre-test caveat; Welch's T-test is usually the safer default unless equal variances are justified externally.

Use \(F_{18,10}\), compare to \(f_{0.025,18,10}\), and since it is not significant, accept equal variances and use pooled T." Identify as many errors or caveats as you can.

question_text: Two independent production lines have known variances \(\sigma_1^2=4\) and \(\sigma_2^2=9\). Samples of size \(n_1=n_2=25\) give \(\bar x_1=20.0\) and \(\bar x_2=18.0\). Test \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1-\mu_2\ne0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: Use \[Z=\frac{(\bar X_1-\bar X_2)-0}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1+\sigma_2^2/n_2}}.\] The standard error is \(\sqrt{4/25+9/25}=\sqrt{0.52}=0.721\). Thus \(z=2.0/0.721=2.77\). Since \(|2.77|>1.96\), reject \(H_0\). The two-sided P-value is approximately \(2(1-\Phi(2.77))=0.0056\). There is strong evidence that the two line means differ.
text_for_embedding:
Two independent production lines have known variances \(\sigma_1^2=4\) and \(\sigma_2^2=9\). Samples of size \(n_1=n_2=25\) give \(\bar x_1=20.0\) and \(\bar x_2=18.0\). Test \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1-\mu_2\ne0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\).

Use \[Z=\frac{(\bar X_1-\bar X_2)-0}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1+\sigma_2^2/n_2}}.\] The standard error is \(\sqrt{4/25+9/25}=\sqrt{0.52}=0.721\). Thus \(z=2.0/0.721=2.77\). Since \(|2.77|>1.96\), reject \(H_0\). The two-sided P-value is approximately \(2(1-\Phi(2.77))=0.0056\). There is strong evidence that the two line means differ.

The standard error uses (sigma_i^2/n_i), not (sigma_i^2).
thought_text: The standard error uses (sigma_i^2/n_i), not (sigma_i^2).

question_text: Two normal populations are believed to share a common unknown variance. Samples give \(n_1=10,\bar x_1=85.2,s_1=4.5\) and \(n_2=12,\bar x_2=82.1,s_2=5.0\). Test \(H_0:\mu_1=\mu_2\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1\ne\mu_2\) at \(\alpha=0.05\) using the pooled T-test.
text_for_embedding:
Two normal populations are believed to share a common unknown variance. Samples give \(n_1=10,\bar x_1=85.2,s_1=4.5\) and \(n_2=12,\bar x_2=82.1,s_2=5.0\). Test \(H_0:\mu_1=\mu_2\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1\ne\mu_2\) at \(\alpha=0.05\) using the pooled T-test.

The pooled variance is \[s_p^2=\frac{9(4.5^2)+11(5.0^2)}{20}=22.86,\quad s_p=4.781.\] The standard error is \(s_p\sqrt{1/10+1/12}=4.781(0.4282)=2.047\). The statistic is \(t=(85.2-82.1)/2.047=1.51\), with \(df=10+12-2=20\). Since \(t_{0.025,20}=2.086\) and \(|1.51|<2.086\), do not reject. The P-value is about 0.15.

Pooling is not averaging (s_1) and (s_2). It is a degrees-of-freedom weighted average of variances.
thought_text: Pooling is not averaging (s_1) and (s_2). It is a degrees-of-freedom weighted average of variances.

question_text: Assume independent normal samples with a common unknown variance. Given \(n_1=16,\bar x_1=42.0,s_1=6.0\) and \(n_2=14,\bar x_2=38.5,s_2=5.0\), construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\).
text_for_embedding:
Assume independent normal samples with a common unknown variance. Given \(n_1=16,\bar x_1=42.0,s_1=6.0\) and \(n_2=14,\bar x_2=38.5,s_2=5.0\), construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\).

First pool: \[s_p^2=\frac{15(36)+13(25)}{28}=30.893,\quad s_p=5.558.\] The standard error is \(5.558\sqrt{1/16+1/14}=2.141\). With \(df=28\), \(t_{0.025,28}\approx2.048\). The interval is \[(42.0-38.5)\pm2.048(2.141)=3.5\pm4.38=[-0.88,7.88].\]

The interval containing 0 agrees with a failure to reject the matching two-sided 5% test.
thought_text: The interval containing 0 agrees with a failure to reject the matching two-sided 5% test.

question_text: Independent samples from approximately normal populations give \(n_1=15,\bar x_1=12.4,s_1=2.0\) and \(n_2=10,\bar x_2=10.1,s_2=3.0\). Do not assume equal variances. Test \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1-\mu_2>0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use Welch degrees of freedom rounded down to 13.
solution_text: Welch standard error: \[\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2}=\sqrt{4/15+9/10}=\sqrt{1.1667}=1.080.\] The statistic is \(t=(12.4-10.1)/1.080=2.13\). With \(df=13\), the one-sided 5% critical value is \(t_{0.05,13}=1.771\). Since \(2.13>1.771\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that \(\mu_1>\mu_2\).

Independent samples from approximately normal populations give \(n_1=15,\bar x_1=12.4,s_1=2.0\) and \(n_2=10,\bar x_2=10.1,s_2=3.0\). Do not assume equal variances. Test \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1-\mu_2>0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use Welch degrees of freedom rounded down to 13.

Welch standard error: \[\sqrt{s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2}=\sqrt{4/15+9/10}=\sqrt{1.1667}=1.080.\] The statistic is \(t=(12.4-10.1)/1.080=2.13\). With \(df=13\), the one-sided 5% critical value is \(t_{0.05,13}=1.771\). Since \(2.13>1.771\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that \(\mu_1>\mu_2\).

For a one-sided alternative, do not use (t_{alpha/2, u}).

question_text: Using the data in Q4 and Welch \(df=13\), construct a 95% two-sided confidence interval for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\).
solution_text: The sample difference is \(2.3\), and the Welch standard error from Q4 is \(1.080\). For \(df=13\), \(t_{0.025,13}=2.160\). Thus \[\mu_1-\mu_2\in 2.3\pm2.160(1.080)=2.3\pm2.33=[-0.03,4.63].\] The interval barely includes 0, so the matching two-sided 5% test would not reject, even though the one-sided test in Q4 rejected.

question_text: Two independent samples have known variances \(\sigma_1^2=16\), \(\sigma_2^2=25\), sizes \(n_1=40\), \(n_2=50\), and means \(\bar x_1=71.4\), \(\bar x_2=68.9\). Find a 95% lower confidence bound for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\).
solution_text: For a one-sided lower bound with known variances, use \[(\bar x_1-\bar x_2)-z_{0.05}\sqrt{\sigma_1^2/n_1+\sigma_2^2/n_2}.\] The standard error is \(\sqrt{16/40+25/50}=\sqrt{0.9}=0.949\). The lower bound is \(2.5-1.645(0.949)=0.94\). A 95% lower confidence interval is \([0.94,\infty)\).

A lower bound subtracts the critical multiple from the observed difference.

question_text: List the key assumptions for the pooled two-sample T-test and explain which assumption is not required by Welch's T-test.
solution_text: The pooled test assumes two independent random samples, population normality for exact small-sample inference, and a common population variance \(\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2\). The null is about the means, but the pooled denominator is valid only under the common-variance model. Welch's test keeps the independent-samples and approximate normality requirements, but it does not require equal variances. That is why it uses \(s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2\) and Welch-Satterthwaite degrees of freedom instead of \(s_p\) and \(n_1+n_2-2\).

Welch is not a paired test. It is still for two independent samples.
thought_text: Welch is not a paired test. It is still for two independent samples.

question_text: A study measures blood pressure before and after a drug in the same 18 people. A classmate proposes a two-sample Welch test comparing the 18 before values and 18 after values. Diagnose the proposal.
solution_text: The proposal is wrong because the two samples are not independent. The observations are paired by subject, so the right analysis is a one-sample T-test on the differences \(D_i=\text{before}_i-\text{after}_i\), assuming the differences are approximately normal or \(n\) is large enough for a T approximation. Welch solves unequal variances for independent samples; it does not repair dependence created by repeated measurements on the same subjects.

Welch solves unequal variances for independent samples; it does not repair dependence created by repeated measurements on the same subjects.

question_text: For \(n_1=20,n_2=12,s_1^2=4.0,s_2^2=15.0\), compute the Welch-Satterthwaite degrees of freedom \[\nu=\frac{(s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2)^2}{(s_1^2/n_1)^2/(n_1-1)+(s_2^2/n_2)^2/(n_2-1)}.\] What d.f. should be used in the test?
solution_text: Compute \(s_1^2/n_1=4/20=0.20\) and \(s_2^2/n_2=15/12=1.25\). The numerator is \((1.45)^2=2.1025\). The denominator is \(0.20^2/19+1.25^2/11=0.00211+0.14205=0.14416\). Thus \(\nu=2.1025/0.14416=14.59\). Following the conservative course convention, round down to \(df=14\).

question_text: Independent normal samples with a common variance have \(n_1=9,\bar x_1=52.4,s_1=3.0\) and \(n_2=11,\bar x_2=49.1,s_2=2.5\). Find a 90% lower confidence bound for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\).
solution_text: Pool the variances: \[s_p^2=\frac{8(9)+10(6.25)}{18}=7.472,\quad s_p=2.733.\] The standard error is \(2.733\sqrt{1/9+1/11}=1.228\). For a 90% lower bound, use \(t_{0.10,18}=1.330\). The lower bound is \((52.4-49.1)-1.330(1.228)=3.3-1.63=1.67\). The confidence interval is \([1.67,\infty)\).

question_text: Two independent samples give \(n_1=8,\bar x_1=102.5,s_1=12.0\) and \(n_2=20,\bar x_2=91.0,s_2=8.0\). Use Welch's test for \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1-\mu_2\ne0\). The Welch d.f. is 9. Use \(t_{0.025,9}=2.262\).
solution_text: The Welch standard error is \(\sqrt{144/8+64/20}=\sqrt{18+3.2}=\sqrt{21.2}=4.604\). The statistic is \(t=(102.5-91.0)/4.604=2.50\). With \(df=9\), \(|2.50|>2.262\), so reject at the 5% level. A two-sided P-value is between 0.02 and 0.05, approximately 0.034.

question_text: An analyst first runs an F-test for equal variances at \(\alpha=0.05\). If it fails to reject, they use the pooled T-test; if it rejects, they use Welch's T-test. Explain why this workflow is discouraged, and state the safer default.
solution_text: The workflow uses the same data twice: first to choose the model, then to test the means as though the chosen model had been fixed in advance. This changes the actual Type I error and power properties of the final procedure. Also, the F-test is sensitive to non-normality and often has low power, so a failure to reject equal variances is weak evidence for equality. The safer default is to use Welch's T-test for independent two-sample mean comparisons unless the equal-variance model is justified by design or strong external knowledge. Welch performs well when variances are unequal and is usually close to pooled T when they are equal.

Also, the F-test is sensitive to non-normality and often has low power, so a failure to reject equal variances is weak evidence for equality. The safer default is to use Welch's T-test for independent two-sample mean comparisons unless the equal-variance model is justified by design or strong external knowledge. Welch performs well when variances are unequal and is usually close to pooled T when they are equal.

question_text: Suppose \(\sigma_1=\sigma_2=6\) are known and equal sample sizes \(n_1=n_2=n\) will be used. How large should \(n\) be in each group so that a 95% confidence interval for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\) has total width at most 4?
solution_text: Total width at most 4 means margin \(E\le2\). For equal \(n\), \(\text{SE}=\sqrt{36/n+36/n}=6\sqrt{2/n}\). Require \(1.96\cdot6\sqrt{2/n}\le2\). Thus \(n\ge 2(1.96)^2(6^2)/2^2=69.15\). Round up to \(n=70\) per group.

question_text: A two-sided Z test of \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\) uses \(n_1=n_2=36\), known common \(\sigma=6\), and \(\alpha=0.05\). Approximate the power when the true difference is \(\delta=4\).
solution_text: The standard error is \(\sigma\sqrt{2/n}=6\sqrt{2/36}=1.414\). The rejection threshold for the observed difference is \(\pm1.96(1.414)=\pm2.77\). Under \(\delta=4\), \(\bar X_1-\bar X_2\sim N(4,1.414^2)\). Power is \(P(D>2.77)+P(D(2.77-4)/1.414)=P(Z>-0.87)=0.807\). Power is about 0.81.

question_text: Using the normal approximation, find the equal per-group sample size needed for a one-sided 5% test to have 80% power to detect \(\mu_1-\mu_2=3\), assuming a common \(\sigma=5\).
solution_text: For a one-sided test, the approximate planning equation is \[\frac{\delta}{\sigma\sqrt{2/n}}\ge z_\alpha+z_\beta,\] where \(1-\beta=0.80\), so \(z_\beta=z_{0.20}=0.842\), and \(z_\alpha=z_{0.05}=1.645\). Thus \[n\ge 2\sigma^2\left(\frac{z_\alpha+z_\beta}{\delta}\right)^2=2(25)\left(\frac{2.487}{3}\right)^2=34.36.\] Round up to \(n=35\) per group.

question_text: A study will sample twice as many controls as treatments: \(n_2=2n_1\). Known standard deviations are \(\sigma_1=10\) for treatment and \(\sigma_2=12\) for control. Find \(n_1,n_2\) so that a 95% CI for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\) has margin at most 4.
solution_text: The standard error is \[\sqrt{100/n_1+144/(2n_1)}=\sqrt{172/n_1}.\] Require \(1.96\sqrt{172/n_1}\le4\). Hence \(n_1\ge 1.96^2(172)/16=41.30\). Round up to \(n_1=42\) treatments and \(n_2=84\) controls.

The standard error is \[\sqrt{100/n_1+144/(2n_1)}=\sqrt{172/n_1}.\] Require \(1.96\sqrt{172/n_1}\le4\). Hence \(n_1\ge 1.96^2(172)/16=41.30\). Round up to \(n_1=42\) treatments and \(n_2=84\) controls.

question_text: A 95% pooled T confidence interval for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\) is \([-1.2,5.6]\). Without recomputing the T statistic, what are the decisions for two-sided 5% tests of \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\) and \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=6\)?
solution_text: For the matching two-sided pooled T procedure, reject a null value if it lies outside the 95% confidence interval. Since 0 lies inside \([-1.2,5.6]\), do not reject \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=0\). Since 6 lies outside the interval, reject \(H_0:\mu_1-\mu_2=6\) at the 5% level.

question_text: For the pooled T-test, show that when \(n_1=n_2=n\), the pooled variance is the simple average of \(s_1^2\) and \(s_2^2\). Does this remain true when \(n_1\ne n_2\)?
solution_text: If \(n_1=n_2=n\), then \[s_p^2=\frac{(n-1)s_1^2+(n-1)s_2^2}{2n-2}=\frac{s_1^2+s_2^2}{2}.\] When \(n_1\ne n_2\), it is not a simple average. The larger sample receives weight proportional to its degrees of freedom, \(n_i-1\). This weighting is needed because each sample variance is based on a different amount of information.

question_text: Two independent samples have \(n_1=120,n_2=150\). Histograms are right-skewed, but neither has extreme outliers. A student says no two-sample T method can be used because the populations are not normal. Diagnose the claim.
solution_text: The claim is too strong. Exact small-sample T theory assumes normality, but for large independent samples the central limit theorem often makes \(\bar X_1-\bar X_2\) approximately normal. A Welch large-sample T procedure is reasonable here, especially because it does not require equal variances. The answer should still check that observations are independent and that there are no extreme outliers or infinite-variance behavior. Skewness alone with \(n_1=120,n_2=150\) is not enough to forbid two-sample mean inference.

question_text: A student writes \(T=(\bar x_1-\bar x_2)/(s_p(1/n_1+1/n_2))\) for the pooled test. Identify the error and explain its effect on the test statistic.
solution_text: The denominator should be \(s_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}\), not \(s_p(1/n_1+1/n_2)\). The square root is essential because variances add, then standard deviation is the square root of variance. Since \(1/n_1+1/n_2<1\) in ordinary samples, omitting the square root makes the denominator too small, inflating \(|T|\) and creating too many false rejections.

question_text: Independent samples give \(n_1=6,\bar x_1=30.0,s_1=10.0\) and \(n_2=30,\bar x_2=22.0,s_2=4.0\). Compare the pooled and Welch two-sided 5% tests of \(H_0:\mu_1=\mu_2\). Use Welch \(df=5\).
solution_text: Pooled: \(s_p^2=[5(100)+29(16)]/34=28.35\), \(s_p=5.324\). SE \(=5.324\sqrt{1/6+1/30}=2.381\), so \(t=8/2.381=3.36\), \(df=34\). Since \(3.36>t_{0.025,34}\approx2.03\), pooled rejects. Welch: SE \(=\sqrt{100/6+16/30}=\sqrt{17.20}=4.147\), so \(t=8/4.147=1.93\), \(df=5\). Since \(1.93<t_{0.025,5}=2.571\), Welch does not reject. The conflict occurs because the small sample has the much larger variance. Pooling lets the large low-variance sample dominate \(s_p^2\), underestimating the uncertainty in \(\bar X_1\).

Welch: SE \(=\sqrt{100/6+16/30}=\sqrt{17.20}=4.147\), so \(t=8/4.147=1.93\), \(df=5\). Since \(1.93<t_{0.025,5}=2.571\), Welch does not reject. The conflict occurs because the small sample has the much larger variance. Pooling lets the large low-variance sample dominate \(s_p^2\), underestimating the uncertainty in \(\bar X_1\).

question_text: Independent samples have \(n_1=18,\bar x_1=14.8,s_1=5.4\) and \(n_2=24,\bar x_2=11.6,s_2=2.7\). Do not assume equal variances. Find a 99% lower confidence bound for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\). Use Welch \(df=22\) and \(t_{0.01,22}=2.508\).
solution_text: The observed difference is \(3.2\). The Welch standard error is \[\sqrt{5.4^2/18+2.7^2/24}=\sqrt{29.16/18+7.29/24}=\sqrt{1.923}=1.387.\] A 99% lower bound is \(3.2-2.508(1.387)=3.2-3.48=-0.28\). The confidence interval is \([-0.28,\infty)\).

Independent samples have \(n_1=18,\bar x_1=14.8,s_1=5.4\) and \(n_2=24,\bar x_2=11.6,s_2=2.7\). Do not assume equal variances. Find a 99% lower confidence bound for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\). Use Welch \(df=22\) and \(t_{0.01,22}=2.508\).

The observed difference is \(3.2\). The Welch standard error is \[\sqrt{5.4^2/18+2.7^2/24}=\sqrt{29.16/18+7.29/24}=\sqrt{1.923}=1.387.\] A 99% lower bound is \(3.2-2.508(1.387)=3.2-3.48=-0.28\). The confidence interval is \([-0.28,\infty)\).

The lower bound can be negative even when the sample difference is positive.
thought_text: The lower bound can be negative even when the sample difference is positive.

question_text: A one-sided pooled T-test of \(H_0:\mu_1\le\mu_2\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_1>\mu_2\) uses \(n_1=n_2=15\), estimated common \(\sigma=3.0\), and \(\alpha=0.05\). Approximate the power if the true difference is \(\delta=3.0\). Use a normal approximation to the noncentral T calculation.
solution_text: The standard error is \(3\sqrt{2/15}=1.095\). With \(df=28\), \(t_{0.05,28}\approx1.701\), so the rejection threshold for \(\bar X_1-\bar X_2\) is \(1.701(1.095)=1.86\). Under \(\delta=3.0\), approximate power is \(P(D>1.86)\) for \(D\sim N(3.0,1.095^2)\). Thus \(P(Z>(1.86-3.0)/1.095)=P(Z>-1.04)=0.85\). The power is about 0.85.
text_for_embedding:

The standard error is \(3\sqrt{2/15}=1.095\). With \(df=28\), \(t_{0.05,28}\approx1.701\), so the rejection threshold for \(\bar X_1-\bar X_2\) is \(1.701(1.095)=1.86\). Under \(\delta=3.0\), approximate power is \(P(D>1.86)\) for \(D\sim N(3.0,1.095^2)\). Thus \(P(Z>(1.86-3.0)/1.095)=P(Z>-1.04)=0.85\). The power is about 0.85.

The exact alternative distribution is noncentral T; the normal approximation is a planning shortcut.
thought_text: The exact alternative distribution is noncentral T; the normal approximation is a planning shortcut.

question_text: The course OC-chart method for the pooled two-sample T-test uses the transformation \(n^*=2n-1\) when \(n_1=n_2=n\). Explain what \(n^*\) represents and how you would use an OC curve to pick \(n\) for a desired Type II error \(\beta\) at a specified standardized difference \(d=\delta/\sigma\).
solution_text: The pooled equal-size test has \(df=2n-2\). The OC-chart convention often indexes curves by a one-sample-like parameter \(n^*=2n-1\), so that \(df=n^*-1=2n-2\). It is a chart-reading device, not the total sample size. To plan \(n\), compute the standardized effect \(d=\delta/\sigma\), choose the curve or abscissa corresponding to the desired test size \(\alpha\), and find the smallest \(n^*\) whose OC value is at most the target \(\beta\). Then convert back using \(n=(n^*+1)/2\) and round up to an integer per group.

To plan \(n\), compute the standardized effect \(d=\delta/\sigma\), choose the curve or abscissa corresponding to the desired test size \(\alpha\), and find the smallest \(n^*\) whose OC value is at most the target \(\beta\). Then convert back using \(n=(n^*+1)/2\) and round up to an integer per group.

A common mistake is to report (n^*) as the per-group sample size.

question_text: Derive the pooled two-sample T statistic under independent normal samples with \(\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2=\sigma^2\). State its degrees of freedom.
solution_text: Under the model, \(\bar X_1-\bar X_2\sim N(\mu_1-\mu_2,\sigma^2(1/n_1+1/n_2))\). Also, \((n_1-1)S_1^2/\sigma^2\) and \((n_2-1)S_2^2/\sigma^2\) are independent chi-squared variables, so their sum is \(\chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}\). Define \[S_p^2=\frac{(n_1-1)S_1^2+(n_2-1)S_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}.\] Then \((n_1+n_2-2)S_p^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}\), independent of the standardized mean difference. Therefore \[T=\frac{(\bar X_1-\bar X_2)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)}{S_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}}\sim t_{n_1+n_2-2}.\]
text_for_embedding:
Derive the pooled two-sample T statistic under independent normal samples with \(\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2=\sigma^2\). State its degrees of freedom.

Under the model, \(\bar X_1-\bar X_2\sim N(\mu_1-\mu_2,\sigma^2(1/n_1+1/n_2))\). Also, \((n_1-1)S_1^2/\sigma^2\) and \((n_2-1)S_2^2/\sigma^2\) are independent chi-squared variables, so their sum is \(\chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}\). Define \[S_p^2=\frac{(n_1-1)S_1^2+(n_2-1)S_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}.\] Then \((n_1+n_2-2)S_p^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n_1+n_2-2}\), independent of the standardized mean difference. Therefore \[T=\frac{(\bar X_1-\bar X_2)-(\mu_1-\mu_2)}{S_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}}\sim t_{n_1+n_2-2}.\]

The pooled statistic is exact only because the two normal samples share the same variance.
thought_text: The pooled statistic is exact only because the two normal samples share the same variance.

question_text: Why does Welch's test use an approximate degrees of freedom instead of \(n_1+n_2-2\)? Give the formula and explain why the result is usually rounded down in hand calculations.
solution_text: When variances are unequal, there is no single common \(\sigma^2\) to estimate, so the pooled chi-squared argument that gives \(n_1+n_2-2\) degrees of freedom no longer applies. Welch uses \[SE^2=S_1^2/n_1+S_2^2/n_2,\] a sum of two separately estimated variance components. The distribution of this estimated standard error is approximated by matching moments to a scaled chi-squared variable. The Welch-Satterthwaite d.f. is \[\nu=\frac{(s_1^2/n_1+s_2^2/n_2)^2}{(s_1^2/n_1)^2/(n_1-1)+(s_2^2/n_2)^2/(n_2-1)}.\] It is rounded down in hand calculations to be conservative: a smaller d.f. gives a larger critical value.
text_for_embedding:

Welch's d.f. is not a count of observations; it is an effective degrees of freedom.

question_text: A report states: "The F-test for equality of variances gives \(P=0.08>0.05\), so variances are equal. We therefore used the pooled T-test and found \(P=0.049\), proving a mean difference." Identify at least three problems with this reasoning.
solution_text: First, \(P=0.08\) in an F-test does not prove equal variances; it only fails to reject equality at the chosen level. Second, using the F-test to decide whether to pool and then treating the selected T-test as preplanned is a data-dependent workflow that can distort error rates. Third, the F-test is sensitive to non-normality and may have poor power, so it is a weak screening tool. Also, \(P=0.049\) does not "prove" a difference; it is evidence against a null under a specified model. A safer analysis would report Welch's T-test by default, plus assumptions and an effect-size confidence interval.

First, \(P=0.08\) in an F-test does not prove equal variances; it only fails to reject equality at the chosen level. Second, using the F-test to decide whether to pool and then treating the selected T-test as preplanned is a data-dependent workflow that can distort error rates. Third, the F-test is sensitive to non-normality and may have poor power, so it is a weak screening tool. Also, \(P=0.049\) does not "prove" a difference; it is evidence against a null under a specified model. A safer analysis would report Welch's T-test by default, plus assumptions and an effect-size confidence interval.
