question_text: For each scenario, choose the best procedure for comparing means and give a one-sentence justification. (i) Two independent small normal samples, equal variances guaranteed by calibration. (ii) Two independent small normal samples, variances visibly different. (iii) Two independent large skewed samples. (iv) Same subjects measured before and after treatment. (v) Two independent samples with known population variances.
solution_text: (i) Use the pooled two-sample T-test or interval, because the common-variance normal model is justified. (ii) Use Welch's T-test, because equal variances are not credible. (iii) Use large-sample Welch T inference, with outlier checks, because the CLT supports the mean difference and Welch avoids equal-variance assumptions. (iv) Use a paired T-test on subject-level differences, not any independent two-sample test. (v) Use the known-variance two-sample Z procedure.

(v) Two independent samples with known population variances.

(i) Use the pooled two-sample T-test or interval, because the common-variance normal model is justified. (ii) Use Welch's T-test, because equal variances are not credible. (iii) Use large-sample Welch T inference, with outlier checks, because the CLT supports the mean difference and Welch avoids equal-variance assumptions. (iv) Use a paired T-test on subject-level differences, not any independent two-sample test. (v) Use the known-variance two-sample Z procedure.

question_text: A new battery is acceptable if its mean lifetime is no more than 2 hours below the old battery. Let \(\Delta=\mu_{\text{new}}-\mu_{\text{old}}\). Independent samples give \(n_1=25,\bar x_1=48.6,s_1=5.0\) for new and \(n_2=25,\bar x_2=50.0,s_2=4.8\) for old. Use Welch \(df=47\). Test noninferiority at \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: Noninferiority hypotheses are \(H_0:\Delta\le -2\) vs. \(H_1:\Delta>-2\). The observed difference is \(-1.4\). Welch SE \(=\sqrt{25/25+23.04/25}=\sqrt{1.9216}=1.386\). The statistic relative to the margin is \(t=[(-1.4)-(-2)]/1.386=0.433\). With \(df=47\), \(t_{0.05,47}\approx1.677\). Since \(0.433<1.677\), do not reject \(H_0\). The data do not establish noninferiority at the 5% level.

A sample difference above the margin is not enough; it must be far enough above the margin statistically.

question_text: A device is practically equivalent to a standard if \(-1<\mu_1-\mu_2<1\). A 90% two-sided Welch confidence interval for \(\mu_1-\mu_2\) is \([-0.6,0.8]\). What conclusion follows from the two-one-sided-tests equivalence logic at \(\alpha=0.05\)?
solution_text: Equivalence at level 5% can be concluded when the entire 90% two-sided confidence interval lies inside the equivalence margin \((-1,1)\). Here \([-0.6,0.8]\) is completely inside \((-1,1)\), so the data support practical equivalence at \(\alpha=0.05\). This is different from failing to reject equality. Equivalence requires positive evidence that the difference is small enough in both directions.

question_text: Two independent laboratories measure impurity concentration. Summary statistics are shown below. Do not assume equal variances. Construct a 95% Welch CI for \(\mu_A-\mu_B\). Use \(df=10\). Lab n mean s A 7 4.82 0.30 B 9 4.55 0.18
text_for_embedding:
Two independent laboratories measure impurity concentration. Summary statistics are shown below. Do not assume equal variances. Construct a 95% Welch CI for \(\mu_A-\mu_B\). Use \(df=10\). Lab n mean s A 7 4.82 0.30 B 9 4.55 0.18

The observed difference is \(0.27\). Welch SE is \[\sqrt{0.30^2/7+0.18^2/9}=\sqrt{0.01286+0.00360}=\sqrt{0.01646}=0.1283.\] With \(df=10\), \(t_{0.025,10}=2.228\). The CI is \(0.27\pm2.228(0.1283)=0.27\pm0.286=[-0.016,0.556]\). The interval barely includes 0.

A visible difference in sample standard deviations is enough reason not to pool unless the design says variances are common.
thought_text: A visible difference in sample standard deviations is enough reason not to pool unless the design says variances are common.

question_text: A final-exam scenario says: "Two teaching methods are compared using scores from 14 students in Method A and 16 different students in Method B. Scores look roughly normal. Sample summaries: \(\bar x_A=78.4,s_A=9.5\), \(\bar x_B=72.1,s_B=5.2\). A student first performs an F-test, gets \(P=0.041\), then uses Welch and reports \(t=2.18,df=16,P=0.044\)." Give a complete diagnosis: design type, appropriate test, whether the pre-test was needed, and the conclusion at 5%.
solution_text: The design is two independent samples, not paired, because the groups contain different students. The roughly normal small-sample setting supports T methods. Since the sample standard deviations are quite different and the F-test rejects equality, pooling is not attractive; however, the F-test pre-test was not needed. Welch should be chosen as the default independent-samples procedure unless equal variances are justified externally. Using the reported Welch result, \(t=2.18\), \(df=16\), \(P=0.044<0.05\), reject \(H_0:\mu_A=\mu_B\) at the 5% level. Report this as evidence that the mean scores differ, with Method A higher in the sample. The conclusion depends on independence, approximate normality, and the validity of the sampling design; it is not proof that the method caused the difference unless the study was randomized.

question_text: Two independent samples have small sizes and visibly skewed distributions. Group A has values \(3,5,8,9\); group B has values \(1,2,4,6,7\). State why the Wilcoxon rank-sum test is a reasonable alternative to a two-sample T-test, then compute the rank-sum \(W_A\).
solution_text: The samples are independent, small, and skewed, so a distribution-free rank comparison is safer than a normal-theory two-sample T-test if the goal is a location comparison. Pool and rank the nine observations: \(1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9\) have ranks \(1,\ldots,9\). Group A values \(3,5,8,9\) have ranks \(3,5,8,9\), so \(W_A=25\).
text_for_embedding:

The samples are independent, small, and skewed, so a distribution-free rank comparison is safer than a normal-theory two-sample T-test if the goal is a location comparison. Pool and rank the nine observations: \(1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9\) have ranks \(1,\ldots,9\). Group A values \(3,5,8,9\) have ranks \(3,5,8,9\), so \(W_A=25\).

Rank-sum is not a paired test. It ignores any artificial ordering of rows and ranks all observations together.
thought_text: Rank-sum is not a paired test. It ignores any artificial ordering of rows and ranks all observations together.

question_text: For a Wilcoxon rank-sum test with \(m=6\) observations in sample 1 and \(n=8\) observations in sample 2, find \(E(W_1)\) under \(H_0\), where \(W_1\) is the sum of ranks in sample 1.
solution_text: Under \(H_0\), the ranks assigned to sample 1 are like a random subset of \(m\) ranks from \(1,\ldots,m+n\). The average rank is \((m+n+1)/2=15/2=7.5\). Therefore \(E(W_1)=m(m+n+1)/2=6(15)/2=45\).

For a Wilcoxon rank-sum test with \(m=6\) observations in sample 1 and \(n=8\) observations in sample 2, find \(E(W_1)\) under \(H_0\), where \(W_1\) is the sum of ranks in sample 1.

Under \(H_0\), the ranks assigned to sample 1 are like a random subset of \(m\) ranks from \(1,\ldots,m+n\). The average rank is \((m+n+1)/2=15/2=7.5\). Therefore \(E(W_1)=m(m+n+1)/2=6(15)/2=45\).

Do not use the mean of sample values. The statistic is a sum of ranks.
thought_text: Do not use the mean of sample values. The statistic is a sum of ranks.

question_text: A rank-sum test has \(m=10\), \(n=12\), and observed rank sum \(W_1=142\). Using the no-ties normal approximation, compute the standardized statistic for a two-sided test.
solution_text: \(E(W_1)=m(m+n+1)/2=10(23)/2=115\). The variance is \(mn(m+n+1)/12=10\cdot12\cdot23/12=230\), so \(SD=\sqrt{230}=15.17\). The standardized statistic is \(Z=(142-115)/15.17=1.78\). For a continuity-corrected answer one could use \(Z=(141.5-115)/15.17=1.75\); either should be stated consistently.

question_text: Six subjects are measured before and after a treatment. The before-minus-after differences are \(2.1,1.5,2.8,0.9,1.7,2.4\). Test \(H_0:\mu_D=0\) vs. \(H_1:\mu_D>0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\), assuming the differences are approximately normal. Use \(t_{0.05,5}=2.015\).
solution_text: The mean difference is \(\bar D=1.90\). The sample standard deviation of the differences is \(s_D\approx0.68\). The paired statistic is \[T=\frac{\bar D-0}{s_D/\sqrt n}=\frac{1.90}{0.68/\sqrt6}=6.84.\] Since \(6.84>2.015\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that the treatment lowers the measurement when differences are before-minus-after.

The mean difference is \(\bar D=1.90\). The sample standard deviation of the differences is \(s_D\approx0.68\). The paired statistic is \[T=\frac{\bar D-0}{s_D/\sqrt n}=\frac{1.90}{0.68/\sqrt6}=6.84.\] Since \(6.84>2.015\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that the treatment lowers the measurement when differences are before-minus-after.

For paired data, the sample size is the number of pairs, not twice the number of pairs.

question_text: A study records each student's score on a quiz before and after a tutoring session. Should the analysis compare two independent samples or paired differences? Explain in one sentence.
solution_text: Use paired differences. Each before score is naturally linked to the same student's after score, so the relevant noise is within-student change rather than between-student variability.

Ask whether row 1 in one column belongs with row 1 in the other column for a scientific reason. If yes, paired.
thought_text: Ask whether row 1 in one column belongs with row 1 in the other column for a scientific reason. If yes, paired.

question_text: For paired differences \(D=-3,1,4,-2,5\), compute the Wilcoxon signed-rank values \(W^+\), \(W^-\), and \(W=\min(W^+,W^-)\).
solution_text: Rank absolute differences \(1,2,3,4,5\) with ranks \(1,2,3,4,5\). Positive differences are \(1,4,5\), so \(W^+=1+4+5=10\). Negative differences are \(-3,-2\), so \(W^-=3+2=5\). Therefore \(W=5\).
text_for_embedding:
For paired differences \(D=-3,1,4,-2,5\), compute the Wilcoxon signed-rank values \(W^+\), \(W^-\), and \(W=\min(W^+,W^-)\).

Rank absolute differences \(1,2,3,4,5\) with ranks \(1,2,3,4,5\). Positive differences are \(1,4,5\), so \(W^+=1+4+5=10\). Negative differences are \(-3,-2\), so \(W^-=3+2=5\). Therefore \(W=5\).

Do not rank the signed values from most negative to most positive. Rank magnitudes, then reattach signs.
thought_text: Do not rank the signed values from most negative to most positive. Rank magnitudes, then reattach signs.

question_text: A bivariate-normal sample of size \(n=18\) has sample correlation \(r=0.50\). Test \(H_0:\rho=0\) vs. \(H_1:\rho\ne0\) using the exact \(t\)-test. Use \(t_{0.025,16}=2.120\).
solution_text: For \(H_0:\rho=0\), \[T=\frac{R\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-R^2}}\sim t_{n-2}.\] Here \(T=0.50\sqrt{16}/\sqrt{1-0.25}=2/0.866=2.31\), with \(df=16\). Since \(2.31>2.120\), reject \(H_0\) at the 5% two-sided level.
text_for_embedding:

question_text: Write the Fisher transformation used to construct a confidence interval for \(\rho\), and give the approximate standard error of the transformed statistic for sample size \(n\).
solution_text: The transformation is \(\operatorname{artanh}(r)=\frac12\ln\{(1+r)/(1-r)\}\). For bivariate-normal data, \(\operatorname{artanh}(R)\) is approximately normal with mean \(\operatorname{artanh}(\rho)\) and standard error \(1/\sqrt{n-3}\).

question_text: Two independent samples have \(m=5\) and \(n=5\). Sample A ranks after pooling are \(6,7,8,9,10\). Test whether A tends to be larger than B. Suppose the exact 5% upper critical value for \(W_A\) is 40. State the statistic and decision.
solution_text: The rank sum is \(W_A=6+7+8+9+10=40\). The one-sided alternative is that A tends to produce larger observations, so large \(W_A\) is evidence against \(H_0\). Since \(W_A=40\) reaches the critical value, reject at \(\alpha=0.05\).

question_text: Pool the values A: \(2,4,4,7\) and B: \(1,4,5,6\). Compute the rank sum for A using average ranks for ties. Then state one caveat about using the usual rank-sum null distribution.
solution_text: The pooled ordered values are \(1,2,4,4,4,5,6,7\). The three 4s occupy ranks 3,4,5, so each gets average rank 4. A has values \(2,4,4,7\), so \(W_A=2+4+4+8=18\). Caveat: ties change the variance and the exact permutation distribution. With many ties, use a tie-corrected variance or preferably an exact permutation calculation; do not blindly apply the no-ties table.

question_text: A paired study with \(n=12\) has mean difference \(\bar D=-3.2\) and \(s_D=4.8\). Construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\mu_D\). Use \(t_{0.025,11}=2.201\).
solution_text: The paired confidence interval is \(\bar D\pm t_{\alpha/2,n-1}s_D/\sqrt n\). The standard error is \(4.8/\sqrt{12}=1.386\), and the margin is \(2.201(1.386)=3.05\). Thus \(\mu_D\in[-3.2-3.05,-3.2+3.05]=[-6.25,-0.15]\). The interval suggests a negative mean difference at the 5% two-sided level.

question_text: Suppose two measurements on each subject have equal variance \(\sigma^2=16\) and within-pair correlation \(\rho=0.75\). Compare the variance of a paired difference \(D=X-Y\) with the variance that would be used by treating the two measurements as independent.
solution_text: For paired differences, \(\operatorname{Var}(D)=\operatorname{Var}(X-Y)=\sigma^2+\sigma^2-2\rho\sigma^2=2\sigma^2(1-\rho)\). Here this is \(2(16)(0.25)=8\). If treated as independent, the corresponding difference variance would be \(2\sigma^2=32\). Pairing reduces the variance by a factor of \(8/32=0.25\), so it can greatly increase power when \(\rho>0\).

question_text: A spreadsheet lists 20 patients receiving drug A and 20 unrelated patients receiving drug B. A student subtracts row 1 of B from row 1 of A, row 2 of B from row 2 of A, and so on, then runs a paired T-test. Diagnose the error.
solution_text: The row alignment is arbitrary; it does not create a scientific pair. The observations are independent groups, so a paired T-test is invalid because it invents within-pair dependence and uses \(n-1=19\) degrees of freedom on artificial differences. Use an independent-samples method such as Welch's T-test, a pooled T-test if equal variances are justified, or a Wilcoxon rank-sum test if a non-parametric comparison is desired.

question_text: Paired differences are \(0,2,-2,4,-1,1\). Explain how the Wilcoxon signed-rank test treats the zero and the ties in absolute values, then compute \(W^+\) and \(W^-\).
solution_text: Drop the zero difference before ranking. The remaining absolute values are \(2,2,4,1,1\). The two 1s get average rank \((1+2)/2=1.5\), the two 2s get average rank \((3+4)/2=3.5\), and 4 gets rank 5. Positive differences \(2,4,1\) contribute \(3.5+5+1.5=10\). Negative differences \(-2,-1\) contribute \(3.5+1.5=5\). Thus \(W^+=10\) and \(W^-=5\), based on effective \(n=5\).

question_text: A paired dataset has differences with one extreme outlier and an otherwise symmetric shape. Compare the paired T-test and the Wilcoxon signed-rank test for this situation.
solution_text: The paired T-test targets the mean difference and assumes the differences are approximately normal, so a single extreme outlier can dominate \(\bar D\) and \(s_D\). The Wilcoxon signed-rank test targets a median/location shift and uses ranks of \(|D_i|\), so it is less sensitive to the outlier, provided the distribution of differences is symmetric about its median.

The paired T-test targets the mean difference and assumes the differences are approximately normal, so a single extreme outlier can dominate \(\bar D\) and \(s_D\). The Wilcoxon signed-rank test targets a median/location shift and uses ranks of \(|D_i|\), so it is less sensitive to the outlier, provided the distribution of differences is symmetric about its median.

Signed-rank is not assumption-free: symmetry of the difference distribution still matters.
thought_text: Signed-rank is not assumption-free: symmetry of the difference distribution still matters.

question_text: A bivariate-normal sample has \(n=35\), \(r=0.48\). Test \(H_0:\rho=0.20\) vs. \(H_1:\rho\ne0.20\) at the 5% level using Fisher's transformation. Use \(z_{0.025}=1.96\).
solution_text: \(\operatorname{artanh}(0.48)=0.523\) and \(\operatorname{artanh}(0.20)=0.203\). The standard error is \(1/\sqrt{n-3}=1/\sqrt{32}\), so \[Z=\sqrt{32}(0.523-0.203)=5.657(0.320)=1.81.\] Since \(|1.81|<1.96\), do not reject \(H_0\) at the two-sided 5% level.

question_text: A sample of size \(n=30\) has \(r=0.62\). Construct an approximate 95% confidence interval for \(\rho\) using Fisher's transformation.
solution_text: \(\operatorname{artanh}(0.62)=\frac12\ln(1.62/0.38)=0.725\). The transformed standard error is \(1/\sqrt{27}=0.1925\), so the 95% margin is \(1.96(0.1925)=0.377\). In Fisher units, the interval is \([0.348,1.102]\). Back-transforming gives \([\tanh(0.348),\tanh(1.102)]=[0.334,0.802]\).

question_text: A scatterplot shows a strong U-shaped relationship between \(X\) and \(Y\). The Pearson correlation is near zero. Should a test of \(H_0:\rho=0\) be interpreted as evidence that \(X\) and \(Y\) are unrelated?
solution_text: No. Pearson correlation measures linear association, and the usual correlation tests are framed for bivariate-normal or approximately linear settings. A U-shaped relationship can have zero linear correlation while showing strong dependence. The correct diagnosis is that the correlation test fails to detect nonlinearity; inspect the scatterplot and use a nonlinear model or another dependence measure.

question_text: For a rank-sum test with \(m=7\) in sample 1, the observed rank sum is \(W_1=62\). Convert this to the Mann-Whitney statistic \(U_1\).
text_for_embedding:
For a rank-sum test with \(m=7\) in sample 1, the observed rank sum is \(W_1=62\). Convert this to the Mann-Whitney statistic \(U_1\).

The conversion is \(U_1=W_1-m(m+1)/2\). Here \(m(m+1)/2=7\cdot8/2=28\), so \(U_1=62-28=34\).

question_text: A paired analysis reports \(D=X-Y\), \(\bar D=-1.8\), \(s_D=2.4\), \(n=16\). Test whether \(X\) is less than \(Y\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Use \(t_{0.05,15}=1.753\).
solution_text: The alternative \(X<Y\) corresponds to \(\mu_D<0\). The statistic is \(T=-1.8/(2.4/\sqrt{16})=-1.8/0.6=-3.00\), with \(df=15\). For a left-tailed test, reject if \(T<-1.753\). Since \(-3.00<-1.753\), reject \(H_0\). There is evidence that \(X\) is smaller than \(Y\).

question_text: In a large-sample Wilcoxon rank-sum test, \(m=12\), \(n=18\), and several observations are tied in blocks of sizes \(4,3,2\). Explain qualitatively how ties affect the variance, and state what you would do if the tie-corrected variance formula produced a negative number.
solution_text: Ties reduce the number of distinct rank arrangements and require adjustment of the rank-sum variance. Standard tie corrections subtract a term depending on the tie-block sizes from the no-ties variance. With extremely coarse or heavily tied data, a formal correction can behave badly and even produce an impossible negative variance. If that happens, do not report a normal approximation with a negative variance. The principled fix is an exact or Monte Carlo permutation test using the observed tied data. If computing resources or course constraints prevent that, report the uncorrected approximation only with an explicit caveat that ties make the approximation questionable.

question_text: Nine paired nonzero differences produce \(W^+=42\) and \(W^-=3\). For a two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank test at \(\alpha=0.05\), suppose the lower-tail critical value for \(W=\min(W^+,W^-)\) is 5. State the conclusion and interpret it.
solution_text: The test statistic is \(W=\min(42,3)=3\). Since \(3\le5\), reject the null hypothesis of zero median/location shift. Because \(W^+\) is much larger than \(W^-\), the direction of the evidence is positive: the paired differences tend to be greater than zero.

question_text: A before/after study on \(n=14\) subjects reports \(\bar x_{\text{before}}=80\), \(\bar x_{\text{after}}=75\), \(s_{\text{before}}=9\), \(s_{\text{after}}=8\). A student runs a pooled two-sample T-test with \(df=26\). The differences have \(\bar D=5\), \(s_D=4\). Identify the error and compute the correct paired T statistic.
solution_text: The error is treating repeated measurements on the same subjects as independent samples. The correct test uses the \(n=14\) paired differences, not the two marginal standard deviations. \[T=\frac{\bar D}{s_D/\sqrt n}=\frac{5}{4/\sqrt{14}}=4.68,\qquad df=13.\] This will be far more significant than the pooled analysis because the paired differences remove subject-level variation.

question_text: A matched-pair design yields equal marginal variance \(\sigma^2=25\) but within-pair correlation \(\rho=-0.20\). Compare \(\operatorname{Var}(D)\) with the variance under independent sampling, and explain what this means for power.
solution_text: The paired difference variance is \(2\sigma^2(1-\rho)=2(25)(1.20)=60\). Independent sampling would have variance \(2\sigma^2=50\). Thus the paired design has 20% larger difference variance, plus it generally uses fewer degrees of freedom than a two-independent-sample analysis with the same total number of observations. Negative pairing hurts power. If the pairing is forced by the design, analyze paired differences anyway; if the matching was optional, it was a poor matching strategy.

question_text: For \(n=12\), \(r=0.58\), test \(H_0:\rho=0\) vs. \(H_1:\rho\ne0\). Compute the exact \(t\)-statistic and explain why Fisher's transformation is not necessary for this null.
solution_text: The exact null test for zero correlation uses \[T=\frac{r\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}=\frac{0.58\sqrt{10}}{\sqrt{1-0.3364}}=\frac{1.834}{0.8147}=2.25,\] with \(df=10\). Fisher's transform is especially useful for confidence intervals and tests of nonzero \(\rho_0\). For \(\rho_0=0\), the \(t\)-statistic has an exact normal-theory null distribution, so it is the direct course method.

question_text: A bivariate-normal sample has \(n=20\) and \(r=-0.70\). Construct an approximate 95% Fisher confidence interval for \(\rho\).
solution_text: \(\operatorname{artanh}(-0.70)=-0.8673\). The standard error is \(1/\sqrt{17}=0.2425\), so the 95% Fisher-scale margin is \(1.96(0.2425)=0.475\). The transformed interval is \([-1.342,-0.392]\). Back-transform: \(\tanh(-1.342)=-0.872\), \(\tanh(-0.392)=-0.373\). Thus \(\rho\in[-0.872,-0.373]\) approximately.

question_text: A dataset of \(n=25\) has \(r=0.72\), but the scatterplot shows one extreme high-leverage point and the other 24 points form a nearly horizontal cloud. Diagnose the correlation test result.
solution_text: The numerical correlation and its P-value are not trustworthy as a summary of the main data pattern. Pearson's \(r\) is sensitive to high-leverage points, and the usual test assumes an approximately bivariate-normal/linear cloud rather than one point driving the association. The correct response is to inspect the point, check data quality, report analyses with and without it, and consider robust or rank-based association measures.

question_text: Two distributions have the same median but very different shapes and spreads. A Wilcoxon rank-sum test rejects. Is it always correct to conclude that the medians differ? Explain.
solution_text: No. The rank-sum test is sensitive to stochastic ordering, not only to median shifts. A clean median-shift interpretation requires the two distributions to have the same shape apart from a location shift. If spreads or shapes differ, rejection may reflect dispersion, skewness, or other distributional differences rather than a median difference alone.
text_for_embedding:
Two distributions have the same median but very different shapes and spreads. A Wilcoxon rank-sum test rejects. Is it always correct to conclude that the medians differ? Explain.

No. The rank-sum test is sensitive to stochastic ordering, not only to median shifts. A clean median-shift interpretation requires the two distributions to have the same shape apart from a location shift. If spreads or shapes differ, rejection may reflect dispersion, skewness, or other distributional differences rather than a median difference alone.

Non-parametric does not mean assumption-free or interpretation-free.
thought_text: Non-parametric does not mean assumption-free or interpretation-free.

question_text: A paired dataset has many differences close to zero, several exact zeros, and a few very large positive differences. The signed-rank test rejects. Compare what the signed-rank test and the sign test are using from the data.
solution_text: The signed-rank test uses both signs and ranks of the nonzero absolute differences, so large positive differences receive large ranks and can strongly affect the result. It also assumes the difference distribution is symmetric for its usual location-shift interpretation. The sign test uses only whether each nonzero difference is positive or negative; it ignores magnitudes and is more robust but less powerful when signed-rank assumptions hold.
text_for_embedding:
A paired dataset has many differences close to zero, several exact zeros, and a few very large positive differences. The signed-rank test rejects. Compare what the signed-rank test and the sign test are using from the data.

The signed-rank test uses both signs and ranks of the nonzero absolute differences, so large positive differences receive large ranks and can strongly affect the result. It also assumes the difference distribution is symmetric for its usual location-shift interpretation. The sign test uses only whether each nonzero difference is positive or negative; it ignores magnitudes and is more robust but less powerful when signed-rank assumptions hold.

If large magnitudes look suspicious, signed-rank may still be too sensitive; the sign test is the more conservative paired non-parametric option.
thought_text: If large magnitudes look suspicious, signed-rank may still be too sensitive; the sign test is the more conservative paired non-parametric option.

question_text: A student reports: "For \(n=18\) and \(r=0.80\), a 95% confidence interval for \(\rho\) is \(0.80\pm1.96/\sqrt{18}=[0.338,1.262]\), so truncate it to \([0.338,1]\)." Identify the errors and give the correct Fisher interval.
solution_text: Errors: the interval was built on the raw correlation scale, it used \(\sqrt n\) instead of \(\sqrt{n-3}\), and truncating at 1 is not a valid fix. Use Fisher's transformation. \(\operatorname{artanh}(0.80)=1.099\). The standard error is \(1/\sqrt{15}=0.2582\), so the transformed margin is \(1.96(0.2582)=0.506\). Fisher-scale interval: \([0.593,1.605]\). Back-transforming gives \([\tanh(0.593),\tanh(1.605)]=[0.532,0.922]\).
text_for_embedding:
A student reports: "For \(n=18\) and \(r=0.80\), a 95% confidence interval for \(\rho\) is \(0.80\pm1.96/\sqrt{18}=[0.338,1.262]\), so truncate it to \([0.338,1]\)." Identify the errors and give the correct Fisher interval.

Errors: the interval was built on the raw correlation scale, it used \(\sqrt n\) instead of \(\sqrt{n-3}\), and truncating at 1 is not a valid fix. Use Fisher's transformation. \(\operatorname{artanh}(0.80)=1.099\). The standard error is \(1/\sqrt{15}=0.2582\), so the transformed margin is \(1.96(0.2582)=0.506\). Fisher-scale interval: \([0.593,1.605]\). Back-transforming gives \([\tanh(0.593),\tanh(1.605)]=[0.532,0.922]\).

Fisher's transform prevents impossible endpoints naturally; never patch an invalid correlation CI by clipping it.
thought_text: Fisher's transform prevents impossible endpoints naturally; never patch an invalid correlation CI by clipping it.

question_text: For each situation choose the best Chapter 24 method and name one key assumption or caveat: (i) two unrelated small samples with ordinal outcomes; (ii) same machines measured before and after calibration with non-normal but symmetric differences; (iii) same machines measured before and after calibration with approximately normal differences; (iv) testing whether a nonzero population correlation equals 0.30.
solution_text: (i) Use Wilcoxon rank-sum; caveat: independent samples and a location/stochastic-order interpretation, with ties handled carefully for ordinal data. (ii) Use Wilcoxon signed-rank; caveat: paired differences should be symmetric and zeros/ties need handling. (iii) Use paired T; caveat: differences, not marginals, should be approximately normal. (iv) Use Fisher transformation; caveat: approximate normality on the artanh scale, typically justified by bivariate-normal data and adequate \(n\).
text_for_embedding:
For each situation choose the best Chapter 24 method and name one key assumption or caveat: (i) two unrelated small samples with ordinal outcomes; (ii) same machines measured before and after calibration with non-normal but symmetric differences; (iii) same machines measured before and after calibration with approximately normal differences; (iv) testing whether a nonzero population correlation equals 0.30.

(i) Use Wilcoxon rank-sum; caveat: independent samples and a location/stochastic-order interpretation, with ties handled carefully for ordinal data. (ii) Use Wilcoxon signed-rank; caveat: paired differences should be symmetric and zeros/ties need handling. (iii) Use paired T; caveat: differences, not marginals, should be approximately normal. (iv) Use Fisher transformation; caveat: approximate normality on the artanh scale, typically justified by bivariate-normal data and adequate \(n\).

Most final questions first test whether you recognize the data structure: independent, paired, or correlation.
thought_text: Most final questions first test whether you recognize the data structure: independent, paired, or correlation.

question_text: A report says: "We matched subjects by age, but because the paired T-test has only \(n-1\) degrees of freedom, we ignored the matching and used a two-sample pooled T-test to get more degrees of freedom. The Wilcoxon rank-sum test gave a similar P-value, so pairing does not matter." Diagnose this reasoning.
solution_text: The reasoning is flawed. If matching was part of the design, the analysis should preserve matched pairs because the target comparison is within matched blocks. Although paired T has fewer degrees of freedom, positive within-pair correlation can greatly reduce the standard error; degrees of freedom alone do not determine power. A rank-sum test on pooled observations also ignores the matching and therefore answers a different question. If normality of differences is doubtful, use a Wilcoxon signed-rank test on paired differences, not an independent rank-sum test.

The Wilcoxon rank-sum test gave a similar P-value, so pairing does not matter." Diagnose this reasoning.

The reasoning is flawed. If matching was part of the design, the analysis should preserve matched pairs because the target comparison is within matched blocks. Although paired T has fewer degrees of freedom, positive within-pair correlation can greatly reduce the standard error; degrees of freedom alone do not determine power. A rank-sum test on pooled observations also ignores the matching and therefore answers a different question. If normality of differences is doubtful, use a Wilcoxon signed-rank test on paired differences, not an independent rank-sum test.

The right non-parametric analogue of a paired T-test is signed-rank, not rank-sum.
thought_text: The right non-parametric analogue of a paired T-test is signed-rank, not rank-sum.

question_text: A survey classifies \(n=120\) students into four commuting categories: walk, bike, bus, and car. Let the category probabilities be \(p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4\). Write the probabilistic model for the count vector and state the parameter constraints.
solution_text: Let \(N_i\) be the count in category \(i\). The count vector has a multinomial distribution, \[(N_1,N_2,N_3,N_4)\sim\operatorname{Multinomial}(120;p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4).\] The constraints are \(p_i\ge0\) for all \(i\) and \(p_1+p_2+p_3+p_4=1\). Because of the sum constraint, only \(4-1=3\) category probabilities are freely variable.
text_for_embedding:
A survey classifies \(n=120\) students into four commuting categories: walk, bike, bus, and car. Let the category probabilities be \(p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4\). Write the probabilistic model for the count vector and state the parameter constraints.

Let \(N_i\) be the count in category \(i\). The count vector has a multinomial distribution, \[(N_1,N_2,N_3,N_4)\sim\operatorname{Multinomial}(120;p_1,p_2,p_3,p_4).\] The constraints are \(p_i\ge0\) for all \(i\) and \(p_1+p_2+p_3+p_4=1\). Because of the sum constraint, only \(4-1=3\) category probabilities are freely variable.

Categorical data questions usually begin by identifying the count vector, not by treating the four counts as independent binomials.
thought_text: Categorical data questions usually begin by identifying the count vector, not by treating the four counts as independent binomials.

question_text: A spinner is claimed to land in categories A, B, C, D with probabilities \(0.10,0.20,0.30,0.40\). In \(n=200\) spins, what expected counts should be used in a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test?
solution_text: For a simple goodness-of-fit test with specified probabilities, expected counts are \(E_i=np_i\). Thus \[E=(200(0.10),200(0.20),200(0.30),200(0.40))=(20,40,60,80).\]
text_for_embedding:
A spinner is claimed to land in categories A, B, C, D with probabilities \(0.10,0.20,0.30,0.40\). In \(n=200\) spins, what expected counts should be used in a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test?

For a simple goodness-of-fit test with specified probabilities, expected counts are \(E_i=np_i\). Thus \[E=(200(0.10),200(0.20),200(0.30),200(0.40))=(20,40,60,80).\]

Expected counts come from the null model. They are not the nearest round numbers to the observations.
thought_text: Expected counts come from the null model. They are not the nearest round numbers to the observations.

question_text: Observed counts in four categories are \(O=(18,44,54,84)\). Under the null model \(p=(0.10,0.20,0.30,0.40)\) with \(n=200\), compute the chi-squared goodness-of-fit statistic.
text_for_embedding:
Observed counts in four categories are \(O=(18,44,54,84)\). Under the null model \(p=(0.10,0.20,0.30,0.40)\) with \(n=200\), compute the chi-squared goodness-of-fit statistic.

The expected counts from the null are \(E=(20,40,60,80)\). The statistic is \[\chi^2=\sum_i\frac{(O_i-E_i)^2}{E_i}=\frac{(18-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(44-40)^2}{40}+\frac{(54-60)^2}{60}+\frac{(84-80)^2}{80}.\] Numerically, \(\chi^2=0.20+0.40+0.60+0.20=1.40\).

Large positive and negative deviations do not cancel in this statistic because deviations are squared.
thought_text: Large positive and negative deviations do not cancel in this statistic because deviations are squared.

question_text: For the statistic in Q3, use \(df=3\) and the 5% critical value \(\chi^2_{0.05,3}=7.815\) in right-tail notation. State the conclusion.
solution_text: The statistic is \(1.40\), which is less than the critical value \(7.815\). Therefore we fail to reject \(H_0\) at the 5% level. There is not enough evidence that the spinner probabilities differ from \((0.10,0.20,0.30,0.40)\). This does not prove that the null probabilities are exactly true.
text_for_embedding:
For the statistic in Q3, use \(df=3\) and the 5% critical value \(\chi^2_{0.05,3}=7.815\) in right-tail notation. State the conclusion.

The statistic is \(1.40\), which is less than the critical value \(7.815\). Therefore we fail to reject \(H_0\) at the 5% level. There is not enough evidence that the spinner probabilities differ from \((0.10,0.20,0.30,0.40)\). This does not prove that the null probabilities are exactly true.

A small chi-squared statistic means observed counts are close to expected counts; it is not evidence against the null.
thought_text: A small chi-squared statistic means observed counts are close to expected counts; it is not evidence against the null.

question_text: A goodness-of-fit test compares observed counts in \(k=6\) categories to fully specified null probabilities. No parameters are estimated from the data. What degrees of freedom should be used, and why?
solution_text: Use \(df=k-1=6-1=5\). The subtraction of 1 comes from the fixed total count: once five category counts are known, the sixth is determined by the sample size.
text_for_embedding:
A goodness-of-fit test compares observed counts in \(k=6\) categories to fully specified null probabilities. No parameters are estimated from the data. What degrees of freedom should be used, and why?

Use \(df=k-1=6-1=5\). The subtraction of 1 comes from the fixed total count: once five category counts are known, the sixth is determined by the sample size.

Do not subtract a parameter unless it is estimated from the same data used in the goodness-of-fit test.
thought_text: Do not subtract a parameter unless it is estimated from the same data used in the goodness-of-fit test.

question_text: A proposed goodness-of-fit test has expected counts \(12.1,8.7,6.4,2.8\). What concern should be raised before using the chi-squared approximation?
solution_text: The expected count \(2.8\) is small. A common course rule is that expected counts should generally be at least 5, or that only a small fraction may fall below 5 with none extremely small, depending on the convention used. The analyst should combine categories in a substantively meaningful way or use an exact/simulation method if allowed.
text_for_embedding:
A proposed goodness-of-fit test has expected counts \(12.1,8.7,6.4,2.8\). What concern should be raised before using the chi-squared approximation?

The expected count \(2.8\) is small. A common course rule is that expected counts should generally be at least 5, or that only a small fraction may fall below 5 with none extremely small, depending on the convention used. The analyst should combine categories in a substantively meaningful way or use an exact/simulation method if allowed.

The rule is about expected counts under the null, not observed counts.
thought_text: The rule is about expected counts under the null, not observed counts.

question_text: A goodness-of-fit test bins \(n=150\) observations into \(k=7\) categories and compares them to a Poisson distribution. The Poisson mean \(\lambda\) is estimated from the same sample before expected counts are computed. What degrees of freedom should be used?
solution_text: For a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test, \[df=k-1-m,\] where \(m\) is the number of parameters estimated from the same data. Here \(k=7\) and \(m=1\) because \(\lambda\) is estimated. Thus \(df=7-1-1=5\).
text_for_embedding:
A goodness-of-fit test bins \(n=150\) observations into \(k=7\) categories and compares them to a Poisson distribution. The Poisson mean \(\lambda\) is estimated from the same sample before expected counts are computed. What degrees of freedom should be used?

For a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test, \[df=k-1-m,\] where \(m\) is the number of parameters estimated from the same data. Here \(k=7\) and \(m=1\) because \(\lambda\) is estimated. Thus \(df=7-1-1=5\).

Estimating (lambda) makes the fitted expected counts closer to the data, so the reference distribution loses one degree of freedom.
thought_text: Estimating (lambda) makes the fitted expected counts closer to the data, so the reference distribution loses one degree of freedom.

question_text: A die is rolled 60 times and a fairness test is planned. Expected counts under fairness are 10 in each face. Suppose a separate manufacturing-defect model instead gives expected counts \((2,5,11,14,13,15)\). Explain whether combining categories is needed for the defect-model test and how it affects degrees of freedom.
solution_text: For the fairness test, all expected counts are 10, so no combining is needed by the usual rule. For the defect-model test, the first expected count is 2 and the second is borderline at 5, so the chi-squared approximation is questionable unless categories are combined. If categories 1 and 2 are combined, the new expected count is \(2+5=7\). The number of categories changes from 6 to 5. If the defect probabilities are fully specified, the degrees of freedom become \(5-1=4\).
text_for_embedding:

For the fairness test, all expected counts are 10, so no combining is needed by the usual rule. For the defect-model test, the first expected count is 2 and the second is borderline at 5, so the chi-squared approximation is questionable unless categories are combined. If categories 1 and 2 are combined, the new expected count is \(2+5=7\). The number of categories changes from 6 to 5. If the defect probabilities are fully specified, the degrees of freedom become \(5-1=4\).

Combining changes the analysis; recompute both the statistic and degrees of freedom using the combined table.
thought_text: Combining changes the analysis; recompute both the statistic and degrees of freedom using the combined table.

question_text: A sensor measurement is tested against \(N(0,1)\). The 200 measurements are binned into \((-\infty,-1],(-1,0],(0,1],(1,\infty)\). Find the expected counts using \(\Phi(-1)=0.1587\) and \(\Phi(1)=0.8413\).
text_for_embedding:
A sensor measurement is tested against \(N(0,1)\). The 200 measurements are binned into \((-\infty,-1],(-1,0],(0,1],(1,\infty)\). Find the expected counts using \(\Phi(-1)=0.1587\) and \(\Phi(1)=0.8413\).

The bin probabilities are \(0.1587\), \(0.5-0.1587=0.3413\), \(0.8413-0.5=0.3413\), and \(1-0.8413=0.1587\). Multiplying by \(n=200\) gives expected counts \[(31.74,68.26,68.26,31.74).\]

For continuous models, expected bin counts come from probability areas, not from density heights at bin centers.
thought_text: For continuous models, expected bin counts come from probability areas, not from density heights at bin centers.

question_text: A normal goodness-of-fit test uses \(k=8\) prechosen bins. The mean and variance of the normal model are estimated from the same data before computing expected counts. What reference degrees of freedom should be used?
solution_text: Use \(df=k-1-m\). A normal distribution has two fitted parameters, \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\), so \(m=2\). Therefore \[df=8-1-2=5.\]
text_for_embedding:
A normal goodness-of-fit test uses \(k=8\) prechosen bins. The mean and variance of the normal model are estimated from the same data before computing expected counts. What reference degrees of freedom should be used?

Use \(df=k-1-m\). A normal distribution has two fitted parameters, \(\mu\) and \(\sigma^2\), so \(m=2\). Therefore \[df=8-1-2=5.\]

Estimating the standard deviation or the variance is one fitted parameter, not two.
thought_text: Estimating the standard deviation or the variance is one fitted parameter, not two.

question_text: A \(2\times3\) table records study method by exam outcome. The row totals are 80 and 120, the column totals are 50, 90, and 60, and the grand total is 200. Under independence, compute the expected count for row 2, column 3.
solution_text: For an independence test, the expected count in cell \((i,j)\) is \[E_{ij}=\frac{(\text{row }i\text{ total})(\text{column }j\text{ total})}{\text{grand total}}.\] Thus \(E_{23}=120(60)/200=36\).
text_for_embedding:
A \(2\times3\) table records study method by exam outcome. The row totals are 80 and 120, the column totals are 50, 90, and 60, and the grand total is 200. Under independence, compute the expected count for row 2, column 3.

For an independence test, the expected count in cell \((i,j)\) is \[E_{ij}=\frac{(\text{row }i\text{ total})(\text{column }j\text{ total})}{\text{grand total}}.\] Thus \(E_{23}=120(60)/200=36\).

Expected counts in contingency tables are built from marginal totals, not from hypothesized category probabilities supplied externally.
thought_text: Expected counts in contingency tables are built from marginal totals, not from hypothesized category probabilities supplied externally.

question_text: For the table below, test statistic setup is requested but no final decision is needed. Low Medium High Total Group A 20 30 10 60 Group B 30 20 40 90 Total 50 50 50 150 Compute the expected counts under independence and the chi-squared statistic.
solution_text: Each column total is 50. For Group A, \(E=60(50)/150=20\) in each column. For Group B, \(E=90(50)/150=30\) in each column. Low Medium High Group A expected 20 20 20 Group B expected 30 30 30 The statistic is \[\chi^2=\frac{(20-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(30-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(10-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(30-30)^2}{30}+\frac{(20-30)^2}{30}+\frac{(40-30)^2}{30}.\] Thus \(\chi^2=0+5+5+0+3.333+3.333=16.667\).
text_for_embedding:
For the table below, test statistic setup is requested but no final decision is needed. Low Medium High Total Group A 20 30 10 60 Group B 30 20 40 90 Total 50 50 50 150 Compute the expected counts under independence and the chi-squared statistic.

Each column total is 50. For Group A, \(E=60(50)/150=20\) in each column. For Group B, \(E=90(50)/150=30\) in each column. Low Medium High Group A expected 20 20 20 Group B expected 30 30 30 The statistic is \[\chi^2=\frac{(20-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(30-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(10-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(30-30)^2}{30}+\frac{(20-30)^2}{30}+\frac{(40-30)^2}{30}.\] Thus \(\chi^2=0+5+5+0+3.333+3.333=16.667\).

A useful check: expected counts must preserve the original row and column totals.
thought_text: A useful check: expected counts must preserve the original row and column totals.

question_text: What degrees of freedom are used for a chi-squared test of independence in a \(4\times5\) contingency table?
solution_text: For an \(r\times c\) contingency table, the degrees of freedom are \((r-1)(c-1)\). Here \[(4-1)(5-1)=3\cdot4=12.\]
text_for_embedding:
What degrees of freedom are used for a chi-squared test of independence in a \(4\times5\) contingency table?

For an \(r\times c\) contingency table, the degrees of freedom are \((r-1)(c-1)\). Here \[(4-1)(5-1)=3\cdot4=12.\]

Do not use (rc-1); the row and column totals impose multiple constraints.
thought_text: Do not use (rc-1); the row and column totals impose multiple constraints.

question_text: A test of independence between phone operating system and preferred news source gives \(p=0.003\). Write an appropriate conclusion and identify one conclusion that would be too strong.
solution_text: At common significance levels such as 5% or 1%, reject independence. The data provide strong evidence of an association between phone operating system and preferred news source in the population represented by the sampling design. A conclusion that is too strong is that the phone operating system causes the news preference. A chi-squared independence test detects association, not causation.
text_for_embedding:
A test of independence between phone operating system and preferred news source gives \(p=0.003\). Write an appropriate conclusion and identify one conclusion that would be too strong.

At common significance levels such as 5% or 1%, reject independence. The data provide strong evidence of an association between phone operating system and preferred news source in the population represented by the sampling design. A conclusion that is too strong is that the phone operating system causes the news preference. A chi-squared independence test detects association, not causation.

Final answers should name the variables and say association, not just write reject (H_0).
thought_text: Final answers should name the variables and say association, not just write reject (H_0).

question_text: A researcher samples 100 people from each of three cities and records whether each person supports a policy. Is the chi-squared procedure best described as a test of independence or a test of homogeneity? State the null hypothesis.
solution_text: This is naturally a test of homogeneity because separate samples are taken from the three cities and the researcher compares the distribution of support across populations. The null hypothesis is that the support distribution is the same in all three cities. With a binary response, that means the support proportion is equal across the three cities.
text_for_embedding:
A researcher samples 100 people from each of three cities and records whether each person supports a policy. Is the chi-squared procedure best described as a test of independence or a test of homogeneity? State the null hypothesis.

This is naturally a test of homogeneity because separate samples are taken from the three cities and the researcher compares the distribution of support across populations. The null hypothesis is that the support distribution is the same in all three cities. With a binary response, that means the support proportion is equal across the three cities.

The computation is the same as an independence test; the study design determines the wording.
thought_text: The computation is the same as an independence test; the study design determines the wording.

question_text: In a contingency table, one cell has \(O=42\) and \(E=30\). Compute its chi-squared contribution and its Pearson residual \((O-E)/\sqrt E\).
solution_text: The contribution is \[(42-30)^2/30=144/30=4.80.\] The Pearson residual is \[(42-30)/\sqrt{30}=12/5.477=2.19.\] This cell has more observations than expected under the null.
text_for_embedding:
In a contingency table, one cell has \(O=42\) and \(E=30\). Compute its chi-squared contribution and its Pearson residual \((O-E)/\sqrt E\).

The contribution is \[(42-30)^2/30=144/30=4.80.\] The Pearson residual is \[(42-30)/\sqrt{30}=12/5.477=2.19.\] This cell has more observations than expected under the null.

The contribution shows size; the residual sign shows direction.
thought_text: The contribution shows size; the residual sign shows direction.

question_text: Counts in five categories are \(n_1,\ldots,n_5=(12,18,25,20,25)\). Write the multinomial likelihood up to the multinomial coefficient and give the MLE of each category probability.
solution_text: Ignoring the multinomial coefficient, the likelihood is \[L(p_1,\ldots,p_5)\propto p_1^{12}p_2^{18}p_3^{25}p_4^{20}p_5^{25},\] subject to \(\sum_i p_i=1\). The total is \(n=100\), so the MLEs are \[\hat p=(0.12,0.18,0.25,0.20,0.25).\]
text_for_embedding:
Counts in five categories are \(n_1,\ldots,n_5=(12,18,25,20,25)\). Write the multinomial likelihood up to the multinomial coefficient and give the MLE of each category probability.

Ignoring the multinomial coefficient, the likelihood is \[L(p_1,\ldots,p_5)\propto p_1^{12}p_2^{18}p_3^{25}p_4^{20}p_5^{25},\] subject to \(\sum_i p_i=1\). The total is \(n=100\), so the MLEs are \[\hat p=(0.12,0.18,0.25,0.20,0.25).\]

The MLEs are observed proportions; that does not mean those same proportions can be used as a null distribution for a GOF test without changing the question.
thought_text: The MLEs are observed proportions; that does not mean those same proportions can be used as a null distribution for a GOF test without changing the question.

question_text: A product is classified as red, blue, or green with probabilities \(0.2,0.5,0.3\). In 10 independent products, write the exact probability of observing counts \((2,5,3)\).
text_for_embedding:
A product is classified as red, blue, or green with probabilities \(0.2,0.5,0.3\). In 10 independent products, write the exact probability of observing counts \((2,5,3)\).

The multinomial probability is \[P(N_1=2,N_2=5,N_3=3)=\frac{10!}{2!5!3!}(0.2)^2(0.5)^5(0.3)^3.\]

The multinomial coefficient counts the number of orders that produce the same unordered category counts.
thought_text: The multinomial coefficient counts the number of orders that produce the same unordered category counts.

question_text: A goodness-of-fit test with \(k=5\) fully specified categories gives \(\chi^2=9.80\). Software reports \(P(\chi^2_4\ge9.80)=0.044\). Give the 5% conclusion in context for a null model of equal category probabilities.
solution_text: With \(k=5\) and fully specified equal probabilities, \(df=4\). The p-value is \(0.044<0.05\), so reject \(H_0\) at the 5% level. There is evidence that the category probabilities are not all equal. The test does not identify by itself exactly which category probabilities differ; inspect contributions or residuals for that.
text_for_embedding:
A goodness-of-fit test with \(k=5\) fully specified categories gives \(\chi^2=9.80\). Software reports \(P(\chi^2_4\ge9.80)=0.044\). Give the 5% conclusion in context for a null model of equal category probabilities.

With \(k=5\) and fully specified equal probabilities, \(df=4\). The p-value is \(0.044<0.05\), so reject \(H_0\) at the 5% level. There is evidence that the category probabilities are not all equal. The test does not identify by itself exactly which category probabilities differ; inspect contributions or residuals for that.

A significant omnibus chi-squared test says the whole vector is inconsistent with the null, not which component caused it.
thought_text: A significant omnibus chi-squared test says the whole vector is inconsistent with the null, not which component caused it.

question_text: Observed and expected counts for five ordered categories are \(O=(3,7,20,32,38)\) and \(E=(4,6,20,30,40)\). If the first two categories are combined, compute the new observed and expected vectors and the new chi-squared statistic.
text_for_embedding:
Observed and expected counts for five ordered categories are \(O=(3,7,20,32,38)\) and \(E=(4,6,20,30,40)\). If the first two categories are combined, compute the new observed and expected vectors and the new chi-squared statistic.

Combining the first two categories gives \(O'=(10,20,32,38)\) and \(E'=(10,20,30,40)\). The recomputed statistic is \[\chi^2=\frac{(10-10)^2}{10}+\frac{(20-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(32-30)^2}{30}+\frac{(38-40)^2}{40}=0+0+0.133+0.100=0.233.\]

The contribution of a merged cell is not generally the sum of the old contributions.
thought_text: The contribution of a merged cell is not generally the sum of the old contributions.

question_text: A student fits a geometric distribution to counts in \(k=6\) categories by estimating the success probability \(p\) from the data. They then use \(df=5\) because there are six categories. Diagnose the error and give the corrected degrees of freedom.
solution_text: The student used the fully specified formula \(k-1\), but the null probabilities were not fully specified before seeing the data. One parameter, \(p\), was estimated from the same sample. The corrected degrees of freedom are \[df=k-1-m=6-1-1=4.\]
text_for_embedding:

The student used the fully specified formula \(k-1\), but the null probabilities were not fully specified before seeing the data. One parameter, \(p\), was estimated from the same sample. The corrected degrees of freedom are \[df=k-1-m=6-1-1=4.\]

A too-large degrees of freedom can make the test less likely to reject than it should.
thought_text: A too-large degrees of freedom can make the test less likely to reject than it should.

question_text: An analyst tests normality by first plotting the histogram, then choosing bin cutpoints that make the observed bin counts look as equal as possible, and finally running a chi-squared normal GOF test. What is wrong with this workflow?
solution_text: The bin cutpoints were chosen after looking at the data to make the observed counts behave in a desired way. The usual chi-squared reference distribution assumes a prespecified grouping rule, or at least a rule not chosen to hide discrepancies. This data-dependent binning changes the distribution of the test statistic and can make the p-value misleading. A better workflow is to choose bins before seeing the data or use a method whose calibration accounts for the bin-selection process.
text_for_embedding:
An analyst tests normality by first plotting the histogram, then choosing bin cutpoints that make the observed bin counts look as equal as possible, and finally running a chi-squared normal GOF test. What is wrong with this workflow?

The bin cutpoints were chosen after looking at the data to make the observed counts behave in a desired way. The usual chi-squared reference distribution assumes a prespecified grouping rule, or at least a rule not chosen to hide discrepancies. This data-dependent binning changes the distribution of the test statistic and can make the p-value misleading. A better workflow is to choose bins before seeing the data or use a method whose calibration accounts for the bin-selection process.

Binning is part of the statistical procedure; it cannot be tuned after the fact without affecting the null distribution.
thought_text: Binning is part of the statistical procedure; it cannot be tuned after the fact without affecting the null distribution.

question_text: A sample of \(n=100\) counts is tested against a Poisson model with \(\lambda\) estimated as \(\hat\lambda=1.2\). Using \(e^{-1.2}=0.3010\), compute expected counts for categories \(0,1,2,3,\ge4\), and state the degrees of freedom after this grouping.
solution_text: For \(X\sim\operatorname{Poisson}(1.2)\), \(P(0)=0.3010\), \(P(1)=1.2(0.3010)=0.3612\), \(P(2)=1.2^2(0.3010)/2=0.2167\), and \(P(3)=1.2^3(0.3010)/6=0.0867\). The tail probability is \(1-(0.3010+0.3612+0.2167+0.0867)=0.0344\). Expected counts are approximately \((30.10,36.12,21.67,8.67,3.44)\). The last expected count is below 5, so combine it with category 3, giving categories \(0,1,2,\ge3\) with expected counts \((30.10,36.12,21.67,12.11)\). After combining there are \(k=4\) categories and one estimated parameter, so \(df=4-1-1=2\).

For \(X\sim\operatorname{Poisson}(1.2)\), \(P(0)=0.3010\), \(P(1)=1.2(0.3010)=0.3612\), \(P(2)=1.2^2(0.3010)/2=0.2167\), and \(P(3)=1.2^3(0.3010)/6=0.0867\). The last expected count is below 5, so combine it with category 3, giving categories \(0,1,2,\ge3\) with expected counts \((30.10,36.12,21.67,12.11)\). After combining there are \(k=4\) categories and one estimated parameter, so \(df=4-1-1=2\).

For count distributions, tail bins are often combined. Decide the final categories before computing the final statistic.

question_text: Use the table below to test independence at the 5% level. The right-tail critical value for \(df=2\) is \(5.991\). Yes No Unsure Total Freshman 25 15 10 50 Senior 15 25 10 50 Total 40 40 20 100
solution_text: Under independence, Freshman expected counts are \(50(40)/100=20\), \(20\), and \(50(20)/100=10\). Senior expected counts are the same: \(20,20,10\). The statistic is \[\chi^2=\frac{(25-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(15-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(10-10)^2}{10}+\frac{(15-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(25-20)^2}{20}+\frac{(10-10)^2}{10}=5.00.\] Degrees of freedom are \((2-1)(3-1)=2\). Since \(5.00<5.991\), fail to reject independence at the 5% level. There is not enough evidence of association between class year and response.

Since \(5.00<5.991\), fail to reject independence at the 5% level. There is not enough evidence of association between class year and response.

question_text: Two teaching methods are compared using independent samples. Outcomes are pass, borderline, fail. Pass Borderline Fail Total Method A 54 18 8 80 Method B 46 22 22 90 Total 100 40 30 170 Compute the expected counts, degrees of freedom, and test statistic for the chi-squared test of homogeneity.
solution_text: Expected counts are row total times column total over grand total. For Method A: \(80(100)/170=47.06\), \(80(40)/170=18.82\), \(80(30)/170=14.12\). For Method B: \(52.94,21.18,15.88\). The statistic is approximately \[\chi^2=\frac{(54-47.06)^2}{47.06}+\frac{(18-18.82)^2}{18.82}+\frac{(8-14.12)^2}{14.12}+\frac{(46-52.94)^2}{52.94}+\frac{(22-21.18)^2}{21.18}+\frac{(22-15.88)^2}{15.88}=7.01.\] Degrees of freedom are \((2-1)(3-1)=2\). The computation is the same as an independence test, but the design makes the interpretation a comparison of outcome distributions between methods.

question_text: A report shows only row percentages for a \(3\times4\) contingency table and then runs a chi-squared test on those percentages as if each row totaled 100. Explain why this can be wrong and what information is needed.
solution_text: The chi-squared statistic is based on counts, not percentages. Treating each row percentage table as if every row has sample size 100 changes the effective sample sizes and therefore changes both expected counts and the test statistic. The original cell counts, or at least the row totals together with row percentages that can reconstruct counts, are needed. A row with 20 observations and a row with 200 observations should not be given the same weight merely because both are expressed as percentages.

question_text: A sample of \(n=160\) is tested against \(N(10,2^2)\) using bins \((-\infty,8],(8,10],(10,12],(12,\infty)\). Observed counts are \(O=(30,52,48,30)\). Using the bin probabilities from \(Z\) cutpoints \(-1,0,1\), compute the chi-squared statistic and degrees of freedom.
solution_text: The bin probabilities are \(0.1587,0.3413,0.3413,0.1587\), so expected counts are \(E=(25.39,54.61,54.61,25.39)\). The statistic is \[\chi^2=\frac{(30-25.39)^2}{25.39}+\frac{(52-54.61)^2}{54.61}+\frac{(48-54.61)^2}{54.61}+\frac{(30-25.39)^2}{25.39}=2.60.\] Because the normal distribution was fully specified as \(N(10,2^2)\), no parameters were estimated. With \(k=4\), \(df=4-1=3\).

If the same data had been used to estimate 10 and 2, the degrees of freedom would be lower.

question_text: A sample is binned into six intervals and compared to a normal model whose \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) are estimated from the sample. The expected counts after fitting are \(3.1,9.5,32.4,31.7,10.2,3.1\). Propose a defensible category combination and give the resulting degrees of freedom.
solution_text: The two tail expected counts \(3.1\) are too small. A defensible combination for ordered normal bins is to merge the left tail with its neighbor and the right tail with its neighbor, producing four categories with expected counts \(12.6,32.4,31.7,13.3\). With \(k=4\) final categories and \(m=2\) estimated normal parameters, the degrees of freedom are \[df=4-1-2=1.\] The same merging must be applied to the observed counts before computing the statistic.

With \(k=4\) final categories and \(m=2\) estimated normal parameters, the degrees of freedom are \[df=4-1-2=1.\] The same merging must be applied to the observed counts before computing the statistic.

question_text: A \(3\times3\) independence table has several expected counts below 5, all in a rare third column. A student deletes the third column and tests independence on the remaining \(3\times2\) table without mentioning the deletion. Diagnose the procedure.
solution_text: The original table violates the usual expected-count condition, so some remedy is needed. However, silently deleting a category changes the population question and discards data, especially because the rare column may be scientifically important. A better response is to combine the rare column with a substantively adjacent or similar category if that is defensible, use an exact or simulation-based method if available, or clearly state that the test is limited to the reduced table. Any changed table must be reported transparently, with degrees of freedom recomputed for the final table.

question_text: A student writes: "The six observed counts are \(14,18,21,15,17,15\), totaling 100. Since the observed proportions are near \(1/6\), I test fairness with expected counts equal to the observed counts. The statistic is 0, so the die is definitely fair." Identify at least three errors and give the correct expected counts for a fair die.
solution_text: Errors include: the expected counts under fairness must come from the null model, not from the observations; using observed counts as expected counts forces the statistic to be 0; failing to reject would not prove the die is fair; and the conclusion should be probabilistic, not definite. For a fair die with \(n=100\), the expected count in each face is \(100/6=16.67\). The correct statistic would compare each observed count with 16.67.

question_text: For a multinomial goodness-of-fit problem, compare the Pearson statistic \(\sum(O-E)^2/E\) with the likelihood-ratio statistic \(G^2=2\sum O\log(O/E)\). What do they have in common, and when should both be treated cautiously?
solution_text: Both statistics compare observed counts with counts expected under the null multinomial model. Under regular large-sample conditions, both are approximately chi-squared with the same degrees of freedom \(k-1-m\), where \(m\) is the number of fitted parameters. Both should be treated cautiously when expected counts are small, when categories were selected after seeing the data, or when the sampling process does not match the multinomial/contingency-table assumptions. For \(G^2\), cells with \(O=0\) are handled using the convention \(0\log(0/E)=0\).

For \(G^2\), cells with \(O=0\) are handled using the convention \(0\log(0/E)=0\).

question_text: For each scenario, choose the correct chi-squared categorical-data procedure and give the degrees-of-freedom formula, but do not compute a statistic. Observed colors are compared to manufacturer-specified probabilities for five colors. Daily accident counts are binned and compared to a Poisson distribution with \(\lambda\) estimated from the data. One random sample records gender and preferred platform in a \(2\times4\) table. Separate samples from three hospitals classify outcomes as improved, unchanged, or worse.
solution_text: (i) Use a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test with fully specified probabilities. The degrees of freedom are \(k-1=5-1=4\). (ii) Use a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test for a fitted Poisson model. If the final number of bins after any combining is \(k\), the degrees of freedom are \(k-1-1=k-2\). (iii) Use a chi-squared test of independence. The degrees of freedom are \((2-1)(4-1)=3\). (iv) Use a chi-squared test of homogeneity because there are separate samples from hospitals. The degrees of freedom are \((3-1)(3-1)=4\).

Observed colors are compared to manufacturer-specified probabilities for five colors. Daily accident counts are binned and compared to a Poisson distribution with \(\lambda\) estimated from the data. One random sample records gender and preferred platform in a \(2\times4\) table. Separate samples from three hospitals classify outcomes as improved, unchanged, or worse.

(i) Use a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test with fully specified probabilities. The degrees of freedom are \(k-1=5-1=4\). (ii) Use a chi-squared goodness-of-fit test for a fitted Poisson model. If the final number of bins after any combining is \(k\), the degrees of freedom are \(k-1-1=k-2\). (iii) Use a chi-squared test of independence. The degrees of freedom are \((2-1)(4-1)=3\). (iv) Use a chi-squared test of homogeneity because there are separate samples from hospitals. The degrees of freedom are \((3-1)(3-1)=4\).

question_text: Write the simple linear regression model for observations \((x_i,Y_i)\), state the meaning of \(\beta_0,\beta_1,\varepsilon_i\), and list the usual assumptions needed for the standard \(t\)-based inferences in this chapter.
solution_text: The model is \[Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_1x_i+\varepsilon_i,\qquad i=1,\ldots,n.\] \(\beta_0\) is the mean response when \(x=0\), if that value is meaningful in context. \(\beta_1\) is the change in the conditional mean response for a one-unit increase in \(x\). The error \(\varepsilon_i\) is the random deviation from the mean line. For the exact \(t\)-procedures, assume \(E(\varepsilon_i)=0\), constant variance \(\operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2\), independence, and normality: \(\varepsilon_i\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\). The \(x_i\)'s are treated as fixed or conditioned upon.

For the exact \(t\)-procedures, assume \(E(\varepsilon_i)=0\), constant variance \(\operatorname{Var}(\varepsilon_i)=\sigma^2\), independence, and normality: \(\varepsilon_i\sim N(0,\sigma^2)\). The \(x_i\)'s are treated as fixed or conditioned upon.

Do not say the observed points lie on the line. The line is for the conditional mean (E(Ymid x)); residuals measure deviations from it.
thought_text: Do not say the observed points lie on the line. The line is for the conditional mean (E(Ymid x)); residuals measure deviations from it.

question_text: Define \(S_{xx},S_{xy},S_{yy}\). Then give the least squares estimators \(b_1\) and \(b_0\) for the fitted line \(\hat y=b_0+b_1x\).
solution_text: The centered sums are \[S_{xx}=\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\bar x)^2,\quad S_{xy}=\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y),\quad S_{yy}=\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\bar y)^2.\] The least squares slope and intercept are \[b_1=\frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}},\qquad b_0=\bar y-b_1\bar x.\] These formulas require \(S_{xx}>0\), meaning not all observed \(x_i\)'s are identical.
text_for_embedding:

The centered sums are \[S_{xx}=\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\bar x)^2,\quad S_{xy}=\sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y),\quad S_{yy}=\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\bar y)^2.\] The least squares slope and intercept are \[b_1=\frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}},\qquad b_0=\bar y-b_1\bar x.\] These formulas require \(S_{xx}>0\), meaning not all observed \(x_i\)'s are identical.

Use centered sums whenever possible; they make the algebra and sanity checks much cleaner.
thought_text: Use centered sums whenever possible; they make the algebra and sanity checks much cleaner.

question_text: A fitted regression of exam score \(Y\) on study hours \(x\) is \(\hat y=52.4+3.1x\). Interpret \(b_0\) and \(b_1\), and compute the fitted value at \(x=8\).
solution_text: The slope \(3.1\) means that the fitted mean exam score increases by 3.1 points for each additional study hour, within the range where the linear model is reasonable. The intercept \(52.4\) is the fitted mean score at zero study hours. It is meaningful only if \(x=0\) is within the relevant range and the linear relationship is plausible there. At \(x=8\), \(\hat y=52.4+3.1(8)=77.2\).

The intercept \(52.4\) is the fitted mean score at zero study hours. It is meaningful only if \(x=0\) is within the relevant range and the linear relationship is plausible there. At \(x=8\), \(\hat y=52.4+3.1(8)=77.2\).

Say 'mean response' or 'fitted response,' not that every individual score rises by exactly 3.1 points.

question_text: For the fitted line \(\hat y=4+2x\), an observation has \(x=6\) and \(y=13\). Compute its fitted value and residual. State whether the point lies above or below the fitted line.
solution_text: The fitted value is \(\hat y=4+2(6)=16\). The residual is \[e=y-\hat y=13-16=-3.\] Because the residual is negative, the observed point lies below the fitted line.
text_for_embedding:
For the fitted line \(\hat y=4+2x\), an observation has \(x=6\) and \(y=13\). Compute its fitted value and residual. State whether the point lies above or below the fitted line.

The fitted value is \(\hat y=4+2(6)=16\). The residual is \[e=y-\hat y=13-16=-3.\] Because the residual is negative, the observed point lies below the fitted line.

The sign convention is important: residual equals observed minus fitted.
thought_text: The sign convention is important: residual equals observed minus fitted.

question_text: Define \(\mathrm{SST}\), \(\mathrm{SSR}\), and \(\mathrm{SSE}\) in simple linear regression. State the decomposition relating them and the degrees of freedom for each.
solution_text: The total sum of squares is \(\mathrm{SST}=\sum(y_i-\bar y)^2=S_{yy}\). The regression sum of squares is \(\mathrm{SSR}=\sum(\hat y_i-\bar y)^2\). The error sum of squares is \(\mathrm{SSE}=\sum(y_i-\hat y_i)^2\). With an intercept in the model, \[\mathrm{SST}=\mathrm{SSR}+\mathrm{SSE}.\] The degrees of freedom are total \(n-1\), regression \(1\), and error \(n-2\).

The total sum of squares is \(\mathrm{SST}=\sum(y_i-\bar y)^2=S_{yy}\). The regression sum of squares is \(\mathrm{SSR}=\sum(\hat y_i-\bar y)^2\). The error sum of squares is \(\mathrm{SSE}=\sum(y_i-\hat y_i)^2\). With an intercept in the model, \[\mathrm{SST}=\mathrm{SSR}+\mathrm{SSE}.\] The degrees of freedom are total \(n-1\), regression \(1\), and error \(n-2\).

The decomposition depends on fitting an intercept. Do not casually use the same identity for no-intercept regression.
thought_text: The decomposition depends on fitting an intercept. Do not casually use the same identity for no-intercept regression.

question_text: Fit \(\mu_{Y\mid x}=\beta_0+\beta_1x\) to the data below. Compute \(S_{xx},S_{xy},S_{yy}\), the least squares line, \(\mathrm{SSE}\), \(s^2\), and \(R^2\). \(x_i\) 1 2 3 4 5 \(y_i\) 2 4 5 7 9
solution_text: \(\bar x=3\) and \(\bar y=5.4\). The centered sums are \[S_{xx}=10,\qquad S_{xy}=17.0,\qquad S_{yy}=29.20.\] Thus \(b_1=17/10=1.7\) and \(b_0=5.4-1.7(3)=0.3\). The fitted line is \[\hat y=0.3+1.7x.\] Use \(\mathrm{SSE}=S_{yy}-b_1S_{xy}=29.20-1.7(17)=0.30\). Since \(n=5\), \(s^2=\mathrm{SSE}/(n-2)=0.30/3=0.10\). Finally, \[R^2=1-\frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{\mathrm{SST}}=1-\frac{0.30}{29.20}=0.9897.\]

question_text: Using Q6's fit, \(b_1=1.7\), \(s^2=0.10\), \(S_{xx}=10\), and \(n=5\). Test \(H_0:\beta_1=0\) against \(H_1:\beta_1\ne0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\), and construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\beta_1\).
solution_text: The standard error is \[\operatorname{SE}(B_1)=\frac{s}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}}=\frac{\sqrt{0.10}}{\sqrt{10}}=0.10.\] The test statistic is \(t=(1.7-0)/0.10=17.0\) with \(df=n-2=3\). Since \(t_{0.025,3}=3.182\), reject \(H_0\). The 95% confidence interval is \[1.7\pm 3.182(0.10)=[1.382,2.018].\]

question_text: A simple linear regression with \(n=18\) observations has \(\mathrm{SSE}=64.0\). Compute the unbiased estimate \(s^2\) of \(\sigma^2\) and the residual standard error \(s\).
solution_text: The regression fits two parameters, so the error degrees of freedom are \(n-2=16\). Therefore \[s^2=\frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{n-2}=\frac{64.0}{16}=4.0,\qquad s=\sqrt{4.0}=2.0.\]

Regression spends two fitted parameters, (b_0) and (b_1), so the denominator is not (n-1).

question_text: A regression has \(n=12\), \(\bar x=10\), \(S_{xx}=80\), \(s=3.0\), and \(b_0=5.5\). Construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\beta_0\). Use \(t_{0.025,10}=2.228\).
solution_text: The standard error of the intercept is \[\operatorname{SE}(B_0)=s\sqrt{\frac1n+\frac{\bar x^2}{S_{xx}}}=3\sqrt{\frac1{12}+\frac{100}{80}}.\] The quantity under the square root is \(0.0833+1.25=1.3333\), so \(\operatorname{SE}(B_0)=3(1.1547)=3.464\). The 95% confidence interval is \[5.5\pm2.228(3.464)=5.5\pm7.72=[-2.22,13.22].\]

question_text: A calibration regression of measured concentration \(Y\) on true concentration \(x\) should ideally have \(\beta_1=1\). A sample gives \(n=15\), \(b_1=0.92\), and \(\operatorname{SE}(B_1)=0.05\). Test \(H_0:\beta_1=1\) versus \(H_1:\beta_1\ne1\) at \(\alpha=0.05\), and give a 95% confidence interval for \(\beta_1\). Use \(t_{0.025,13}=2.160\).
solution_text: The test statistic is \[t=\frac{0.92-1}{0.05}=-1.60,\qquad df=13.\] Because \(|-1.60|<2.160\), do not reject \(H_0\) at the 5% level. The data do not show a significant departure from slope 1. The confidence interval is \[0.92\pm2.160(0.05)=[0.812,1.028].\] It contains 1, agreeing with the test.

question_text: A sample of \(n=20\) has \(\sum x_i=120\), \(\sum y_i=240\), \(\sum x_i^2=820\), \(\sum y_i^2=3260\), and \(\sum x_iy_i=1660\). Compute \(S_{xx},S_{xy},S_{yy}\). Then decide whether these summaries are internally consistent for a real dataset.
solution_text: \(\bar x=120/20=6\) and \(\bar y=240/20=12\). Thus \[S_{xx}=820-20(6^2)=100,\quad S_{xy}=1660-20(6)(12)=220,\quad S_{yy}=3260-20(12^2)=380.\] However, the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality requires \(S_{xy}^2\le S_{xx}S_{yy}\). Here \[220^2=48400>100(380)=38000.\] So the given summary statistics are impossible for any real dataset. If one tried to compute \(R^2=S_{xy}^2/(S_{xx}S_{yy})\), it would exceed 1.

question_text: For a simple linear regression with an intercept, suppose \(S_{xx}=25\), \(S_{yy}=100\), and \(S_{xy}=-40\). Compute \(b_1\), the sample correlation \(r\), and \(R^2\). Interpret the sign of the relationship.
solution_text: The slope is \(b_1=S_{xy}/S_{xx}=-40/25=-1.6\). The sample correlation is \[r=\frac{S_{xy}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}}=\frac{-40}{\sqrt{25(100)}}=\frac{-40}{50}=-0.8.\] Thus \(R^2=r^2=0.64\). The relationship is negative: larger \(x\) values are associated with smaller fitted mean \(Y\) values.

The slope is \(b_1=S_{xy}/S_{xx}=-40/25=-1.6\). The sample correlation is \[r=\frac{S_{xy}}{\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}}=\frac{-40}{\sqrt{25(100)}}=\frac{-40}{50}=-0.8.\] Thus \(R^2=r^2=0.64\). The relationship is negative: larger \(x\) values are associated with smaller fitted mean \(Y\) values.

question_text: In simple linear regression with an intercept, state two residual identities that hold for the least squares fit. Why are they useful for checking arithmetic?
solution_text: The least squares residuals satisfy \[\sum_{i=1}^n e_i=0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n x_ie_i=0.\] Equivalently, the residual vector is orthogonal to the fitted intercept column and the predictor column. These identities are useful because a hand-computed set of fitted values and residuals should nearly satisfy both, up to rounding.

The least squares residuals satisfy \[\sum_{i=1}^n e_i=0,\qquad \sum_{i=1}^n x_ie_i=0.\] Equivalently, the residual vector is orthogonal to the fitted intercept column and the predictor column. These identities are useful because a hand-computed set of fitted values and residuals should nearly satisfy both, up to rounding.

These are exact for the fitted model with an intercept before rounding. If they fail badly, some residual or coefficient is wrong.

question_text: Let \(z_i=x_i-\bar x\) and regress \(Y\) on \(z\) instead of \(x\). Show that the slope is unchanged and that the new intercept equals \(\bar y\).
solution_text: Because \(\bar z=0\), \[S_{zz}=\sum(z_i-0)^2=S_{xx},\qquad S_{zy}=\sum z_i(y_i-\bar y)=S_{xy}.\] Therefore the centered-predictor slope is \(b_1^{(z)}=S_{zy}/S_{zz}=S_{xy}/S_{xx}=b_1\). The intercept in the \(z\)-model is \[b_0^{(z)}=\bar y-b_1^{(z)}\bar z=\bar y.\] Predictions and residuals do not change; only the parameterization changes.

The intercept in the \(z\)-model is \[b_0^{(z)}=\bar y-b_1^{(z)}\bar z=\bar y.\] Predictions and residuals do not change; only the parameterization changes.

Centering is often the fastest way to make the intercept interpretable.
thought_text: Centering is often the fastest way to make the intercept interpretable.

question_text: In an SLR with \(n=12\), \(\mathrm{SSE}=22.5\). Construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\sigma^2\). Use \(\chi^2_{0.025,10}=20.48\) and \(\chi^2_{0.975,10}=3.247\), where subscripts denote right-tail areas.
solution_text: The pivot is \[\frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{\sigma^2}=\frac{(n-2)s^2}{\sigma^2}\sim\chi^2_{n-2}.\] Here \(df=10\). Under the stated right-tail notation, \[\sigma^2\in\left[\frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{\chi^2_{0.025,10}},\frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{\chi^2_{0.975,10}}\right]=\left[\frac{22.5}{20.48},\frac{22.5}{3.247}\right]=[1.10,6.93].\]

question_text: A regression with \(n=25\) has \(R^2=0.716\). Compute the ANOVA \(F\) statistic for testing \(H_0:\beta_1=0\), and state its relationship to the slope \(t\) statistic.
solution_text: For SLR with one predictor, \[F=\frac{\mathrm{MSR}}{\mathrm{MSE}}=\frac{R^2/1}{(1-R^2)/(n-2)}=\frac{(n-2)R^2}{1-R^2}.\] Thus \[F=\frac{23(0.716)}{1-0.716}=\frac{16.468}{0.284}=57.99.\] For testing \(H_0:\beta_1=0\), \(F_{1,n-2}=T_{n-2}^2\). The \(F\) test and the two-sided slope \(t\) test give the same \(p\)-value.
text_for_embedding:
